Assuming “bubble burst” means a broad, sharp valuation reset in AI-linked stocks and startups because realized profits lag today’s expectations—not “AI stops mattering”—my single best estimate is **55%** over the next five years.

Why it’s that high: valuations in parts of AI assume very large future profits; spending on chips, data centers, and models is running ahead of clearly proven monetization; competition may commoditize parts of the stack; and macro, rate, or regulatory shocks could quickly reverse sentiment. History also suggests major platform technologies often go through bubbles before their long-term value is realized.

Why it’s not higher: AI is not pure hype. It already has real use cases, real revenue, and real productivity gains, and the biggest AI spenders are unusually well-capitalized, which can delay or soften a collapse.

So: **55%**. Slightly more likely than not. The most likely “burst” would be a dot-com-style valuation correction, not the failure of AI as a technology.

That assumes “the AI bubble bursts” means a **clear, broad repricing** within five years: AI-linked stocks and private valuations fall sharply, funding tightens, and AI capex gets cut because near-term returns disappoint.

- **Expectations are extremely elevated** relative to what many AI products have proven economically.
- There is a real risk of **datacenter/compute overbuild**.
- A lot of application-layer AI still has **unclear ROI**.
- Models may **commoditize faster than moats form**, hurting margins and valuations.
- A recession, persistently high rates, regulation, copyright liability, or energy constraints could trigger a reset.

Why it’s not higher:
- Unlike many classic bubbles, much of the spending is by **cash-rich incumbents** with real businesses.
- AI demand is already **real, large, and growing**.
- The infrastructure has genuine utility even if some expectations are too optimistic.
- Governments and major platforms may keep spending high for **strategic reasons**, cushioning a collapse.

So: **serious bubble risk, but less than an even-money call on a full-blown bust.**

rows.answer:
If by “burst” you mean a sharp, sustained repricing of AI-linked assets—roughly a 40%+ drop in many AI-related valuations plus a clear funding retrenchment—my single best estimate is **60%** over the next five years.

Why that high:
- **Expectations are extremely elevated.** Markets are pricing in very large, fairly near-term gains.
- **Capex is huge, but monetization is less proven.** If returns on AI spending lag, sentiment can reverse fast.
- **Competition may commoditize the value.** AI can be transformative while still making many current business models less profitable than investors expect.
- **History matters.** Big technological shifts often produce real long-term winners *and* a medium-term investment bust.

Why not higher:
- **AI is already useful and generating real revenue**, unlike many purely speculative manias.
- **The biggest AI spenders and beneficiaries are financially strong incumbents**, not mostly fragile startups.
- **A lot of value may persist even if hype cools**, which would make the unwind more of a correction than a full crash.

So: **60%**.  
That is a probability of an investment/hype bust, not a claim that AI itself will fail.

- **Expectations are very front-loaded.** Markets are pricing in enormous future adoption, margins, and productivity gains.

- **Many AI startups have weak moats.** A lot of value is sitting on uncertain business models, commoditizing models, and high compute costs.
- **History matters.** Real transformative technologies often still produce bubbles before the long-run value shows up.

- **The excess may deflate gradually rather than “burst.”** Fundamentals could catch up enough to avoid a dramatic collapse.

So: **more likely than not, but not a near-certainty.**  
**Best estimate: 60%.**

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 55%.**

Assuming by “burst” you mean a **broad, sustained repricing of AI-linked valuations and spending expectations**—not just a normal pullback—I’d put it at **slightly more likely than not** over the next five years.

Why that high:
- **Investment is racing ahead of proven monetization.** AI capex, chips, data centers, and startup funding are enormous relative to clearly established long-term profits.
- **Real technologies still have bubbles.** Railways, radio, and the internet were all transformative and still had painful busts.
- **Competition may crush margins.** Open-source models, price wars, and weak moats in many AI applications make current expectations vulnerable.
- **Macro and policy could trigger a reset.** Higher rates, recession, power constraints, copyright/regulatory hits, or geopolitics could force a repricing.

Why not higher:
- **AI already has genuine utility.** This is not pure vapor; it is already improving productivity in several domains.
- **The biggest AI spenders are strong incumbents.** Much of the capital is coming from cash-rich tech giants and governments, not only fragile startups.
- **A shakeout is more likely than a total collapse.** The likeliest bad outcome is “AI is real, but near-term expectations overshot,” not “AI was fake.”

So my single-number forecast is **55%**.

rows.answer:
If by “burst” you mean a clear, sector-wide repricing of AI-related valuations and funding—roughly a 40%+ drop in leading AI-linked assets plus a sharp pullback in capital—I’d put the probability at **45%** over the next five years.

Why that high:
- **Valuations and capex are running ahead of proven end-user monetization** in many parts of the stack.
- **History favors boom-bust cycles** for real but overhyped general-purpose technologies.
- **Competition and commoditization** could crush margins even if AI adoption keeps growing.
- **Macro, regulation, copyright, and energy constraints** could all trigger a reset.

Why not higher:
- **AI is genuinely useful already**; this is not purely narrative.
- The biggest spenders are **cash-rich incumbents**, not mostly fragile startups funded by cheap debt.
- Governments and large enterprises increasingly view AI as **strategic infrastructure**, which supports spending through downturns.
- A lot of the likely outcome is **deflation rather than explosion**: weaker players fail, valuations compress, but the technology keeps advancing.

So my single best estimate is: **45%**.

rows.answer:
Best estimate: **60%**.

If by “the AI bubble bursts” you mean a broad, sustained repricing of AI-linked valuations and funding—rather than AI technology itself failing—then 60% is my single best estimate for the next five years.

Why it’s above 50%:
- **Expectations are extremely high.** Current valuations and capex imply very large future profits, and real ROI still has to catch up.
- **Too much money may be chasing too few durable profits.** Many AI products risk commoditization, especially at the model and app layers.
- **History favors boom-bust cycles.** Big general-purpose technologies often produce real long-term value but still go through a financial bubble and reset.
- **There are several credible triggers.** Slower adoption, weaker enterprise willingness to pay, margin compression, regulation, copyright issues, power constraints, or a macro slowdown could all force a repricing.

rows.answer:
My best estimate is **60%** — interpreting “burst” as a broad, sustained repricing of AI-related valuations and funding, not AI itself proving worthless.

Why that’s more likely than not:
- Expectations and capital spending appear ahead of clearly proven profit pools in many parts of AI.
- Much of the stack may commoditize faster than investors expect, which would compress margins.
- The trade is crowded and narrative-driven, so disappointment on adoption, ROI, regulation, power/compute constraints, or macro conditions could trigger a sharp reset.
- Historically, transformative technologies often still go through overinvestment and painful shakeouts.

Why it’s not higher:
- AI already has real utility and strong demand.
- The biggest spenders and likely winners are cash-rich incumbents, not just fragile startups.
- There is a real chance that earnings and productivity gains grow into a lot of today’s optimism.

Bottom line: a meaningful AI shakeout within five years is more likely than not, but the most likely version is “valuations reset while the technology keeps advancing,” not “AI was a mirage.”

rows.answer:
If by “AI bubble” you mean a broad, sentiment-driven repricing of AI-related stocks, startup valuations, and spending plans—not AI progress literally stopping—my single best estimate is **55%**.

Why it’s above 50%:
- Expectations are extremely high relative to what’s already proven in profits and productivity.
- AI capex is massive, and history says big overbuilds often end in a painful reset.
- Many AI application businesses may be commoditized, with value concentrating in a few platforms while weaker firms collapse.
- A recession, higher rates, regulation, lawsuits, or slower-than-hoped adoption could puncture the narrative quickly.

Why it’s not much higher:
- Unlike classic bubbles, a lot of AI spending is being led by very profitable incumbents with real cash flow.
- AI already has genuine utility and real revenue, so this is not purely speculative.
- Even if current expectations are too high, fundamentals may catch up enough to produce a “deflation” rather than a violent “burst.”

So: **55%**. More likely than not, but not overwhelmingly so. And even if the bubble bursts, that would more likely mean a dot-com-style valuation shakeout than “AI was fake.”

Based on information available to me through mid-2024, my single best estimate is **55%**.

That assumes “the AI bubble bursts” means a **clear, sustained repricing** of AI-linked assets and expectations—roughly a 30%+ decline across many AI-exposed valuations, plus a noticeable pullback in funding and AI capex.

Why slightly above even odds:
- **For a burst:** valuations and spending are running ahead of proven monetization in many areas; general-purpose technologies often get overbuilt before economics catch up; startup pricing looks especially vulnerable; any slowdown in model progress, enterprise adoption, or macro conditions could trigger a reset.
- **Against a burst:** AI already has real utility, real revenue, and strong backing from highly profitable incumbents; this is not as purely speculative as the late-1990s internet boom; some valuations could grow into themselves rather than collapse.

So my base case is: **AI remains important and commercially real, but there’s a better-than-even chance the investment story gets sharply repriced within five years.**

rows.answer:
If by “AI bubble” you mean a sharp, sustained repricing of AI-linked public and private valuations, plus a clear cooling of funding enthusiasm, my single best estimate for the next five years is **60%**.

Why I put it above even odds:
- Expectations are extremely high, and a lot of current valuation assumes fast, durable monetization.
- AI capex on chips, data centers, and power is enormous relative to what’s already proven in end-customer willingness to pay.
- Many parts of the stack look vulnerable to commoditization, which could compress margins and undermine startup moats.
- A macro slowdown, regulation, trade restrictions, or infrastructure bottlenecks could trigger a fast sentiment reset.
- Historically, genuinely transformative technologies often still go through bubbles that burst.

Why I don’t put it higher:
- AI is already producing real revenue and real productivity gains; this is not pure hype.
- The biggest spenders are mostly cash-rich incumbents and hyperscalers, not weak speculative companies.
- Falling model and inference costs could let fundamentals catch up to some of today’s optimism.
- Governments and large enterprises increasingly treat AI as strategic, which can sustain spending even through disappointments.

So: more likely than not, but not inevitable. The most likely “burst” would be a valuation reset and funding washout in weaker AI names, not AI as a technology failing.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 60%.**

Assuming “the AI bubble bursts” means a **broad, sustained repricing of AI-linked public and private valuations**—not just normal volatility—I’d put the odds over the next five years at **about 60%**.

Why that’s above 50%:
- **Valuations and capex already imply very high expectations.** Even strong AI adoption may not justify all current pricing.
- **Monetization is real but still uneven.** Many firms are spending heavily before proving durable returns.
- **Too many layers are being priced as big winners at once**: chips, clouds, foundation models, apps, infrastructure.
- **History favors a shakeout.** Transformative technologies often do create real value, but investor enthusiasm still overshoots.

Why it’s not higher:
- **AI is not purely speculative.** It already has genuine utility and revenue.
- **The main players are unusually strong incumbents** with cash flow and balance sheets that can survive a downturn.
- **Costs are falling**, which can let adoption catch up to some of today’s optimism.

So my base case is: **more likely than not that the AI bubble bursts within five years, but as a sharp valuation reset—not as proof that AI itself was a fad.**

rows.answer:
My single best estimate is **40%**.

That assumes “the AI bubble bursts” means something like **AI-related public and private valuations falling roughly 50%+ from peak and staying materially depressed for at least a year because expectations outrun economics**, not just a normal correction.

Why 40%, not much lower:
- **Valuations already price in huge future profits.** A lot has to go right on monetization, margins, and defensibility.
- **Capex is running ahead of proven demand.** If enterprise spending or consumer willingness to pay lags, markets could re-rate sharply.
- **Model economics may commoditize.** Open-source progress, falling model costs, and intense competition could compress returns, especially for frontier labs.
- **Five years is a long time.** A recession, rates shock, regulation, copyright/legal setbacks, or safety incident is very plausible in that window.
- **History is unfriendly to hype cycles.** Transformative technologies often do change the world, but still suffer major valuation crashes on the way.

Why 40%, not much higher:
- **This is not pure vapor.** AI already has real use cases, real revenue, and real productivity value.
- **The biggest buyers are cash-rich firms and governments,** not just retail speculators.
- **Strategic importance matters.** Even if financial returns disappoint temporarily, national-security and platform-control incentives can keep money flowing.
- **A gradual deflation is possible.** Expectations can cool without a dramatic “burst.”

If your specific question is really about **Anthropic**, I’d add one important caveat: **the risk to frontier-model private valuations is higher than the risk to “AI” as a whole**. Even if AI keeps growing, a private lab can still be a bad investment because of dilution, governance, unclear exit timing, and uncertain value capture.

But for the broad question you asked, my best estimate is: **40%**.

**Best estimate: 45%.**

I’m interpreting “AI bubble bursts” as a broad valuation reset and funding retrenchment across AI companies and infrastructure, not just a normal market pullback.

- Expectations and valuations are still ahead of proven profits.
- AI capex is enormous, and the payoff horizon is uncertain.
- Frontier-model businesses may face margin compression from competition, open source, and falling model prices.
- A recession, regulation, legal shock, safety incident, or slowdown in capability progress could flip sentiment quickly.
- Historically, transformative technologies often have real bubbles that burst before the eventual winners dominate.

- AI already has real utility and growing adoption.
- The biggest backers are cash-rich tech giants and governments, not just fragile startups.
- Costs are falling, which can expand real usage fast enough for reality to catch up with some of today’s enthusiasm.
- The sector could deflate gradually rather than “burst.”

So my single best-estimate probability is **45%**.  
For an Anthropic investment, I’d worry almost as much about **entry valuation and deal terms** as about whether AI as a whole keeps booming.

rows.answer:
If by **“the AI bubble bursts”** you mean a **broad, sustained repricing** of AI assets—e.g. major AI equities and private AI company valuations fall ~50%+, funding tightens sharply, and sentiment flips from euphoria to skepticism—then my **single best estimate is 55% within the next five years**.

Why it’s above 50%:
- **Expectations are extremely high.** Current pricing in many AI-related assets assumes huge future profits, not just strong growth.
- **Monetization may lag hype.** AI usage is real, but willingness to pay, retention, and durable margins are still uncertain in many segments.
- **Capital intensity is enormous.** Frontier-model development requires huge ongoing spend; that makes the sector vulnerable if growth slows or financing tightens.
- **Commoditization risk is real.** Model performance is improving broadly, open-source is advancing, and moats may accrue more to chips/cloud/distribution than to frontier labs themselves.
- **Historical base rates are bad.** Truly important technologies often still go through bubbles and crashes.

Why it’s not much higher:
- **AI is already delivering real value**, unlike many past manias at comparable stages.
- **Big-tech and sovereign backers can keep funding the sector** even through a correction.
- **Supply constraints and ongoing capability gains** can keep the boom alive longer than skeptics expect.
- Even if valuations reset, **AI development itself is unlikely to stop**.

So: **55%**.

One extra note: even if the bubble *doesn’t* burst, **Anthropic is not a pure “first-to-AGI = great investment” bet**; valuation, dilution, governance, and whether profits accrue to model labs vs. infrastructure/distribution layers matter a lot.

If by “the AI bubble bursts” you mean a sharp, sustained valuation reset in AI—serious enough to hammer frontier-lab valuations and tighten funding materially—my single best estimate is **45%** over the next five years.

Why it’s that high:

- Compute, data-center, and talent spending is enormous relative to proven end-customer ROI.
- Frontier-model economics may be weaker than they look if open-source, fast followers, or hyperscalers compress margins.
- Historically, genuine technological revolutions still often produce bubbles and painful repricings before the eventual winners are clear.

Why it’s not higher:
- AI already has real utility and real revenue; this is not pure story-stock speculation.
- Much of the spending is backed by cash-rich incumbents, which makes the system more resilient than a bubble built mostly on weak balance sheets.
- Costs keep falling, which can let fundamentals catch up to expectations.
- The “bubble” could deflate gradually rather than burst abruptly.

So my best estimate is **45%**.

One extra caution: even if Anthropic is first to AGI, that does **not** guarantee the best investor outcome. Bubble timing, dilution, partner economics, governance constraints, and pricing pressure could matter almost as much as technical leadership.

rows.answer:
If by “the AI bubble bursts” you mean a sharp, sustained reset in AI-related valuations and funding expectations — not that AI stops being important — my single best estimate is **60%**.

Why it’s more likely than not:
- Current pricing often assumes both very fast capability gains and very large profits for frontier labs.
- AI capex is enormous relative to clearly proven end-user willingness to pay.
- Model advantages may be less durable than bulls expect; competition and open source can compress margins.
- A slowdown in progress, weak enterprise ROI, regulation, copyright liability, or a macro recession could all trigger a repricing.
- Historically, major platform shifts often have a valuation bust before the technology fully wins.

Why it’s not much higher:
- AI already has real use cases and real revenue, unlike many past bubbles at similar hype levels.
- The spending is heavily driven by cash-rich hyperscalers and governments, which can keep the boom going longer than skeptics expect.
- It’s possible earnings and adoption catch up to the hype instead of the hype collapsing.

So: **60%**.

One extra caution for your thesis: even if Anthropic reaches AGI first, that still doesn’t guarantee a great investment outcome; value can leak to cloud providers, chipmakers, customers, or later financing rounds.

rows.answer:
Not investment advice, but if by “AI bubble burst” you mean a broad, recognized repricing — roughly **50%+ declines in leading AI-linked valuations plus a major slowdown in funding/spending** — my **single best-estimate probability is 45% within the next five years**.

Why it’s that high:
- **Valuations are pricing in enormous future profits** that may not materialize on schedule.
- **Capex is running ahead of proven monetization** in several parts of the stack.
- **Frontier-model economics are still unclear**: training/inference are expensive, and competition or open models could compress margins.
- **History**: genuinely transformative technologies often still go through bubbles and painful resets.

Why it’s not higher:
- **AI already has real demand and real revenue**, unlike many classic bubbles.
- The biggest spenders are **cash-rich hyperscalers**, not just fragile startups funded by cheap money.
- A lot of “bubble” pressure could be released through **time and earnings catch-up**, not necessarily a crash.
- If capabilities keep improving materially, markets may tolerate today’s stretched expectations.

So my base case is **no full-blown burst**, but the odds are **close to a coin flip**.

One extra caution: even if the AI bubble does **not** burst, an **Anthropic investment can still disappoint** — because “first to AGI” does not automatically mean “best shareholder outcome” once you factor in dilution, governance, competition, and who actually captures the value.

If by “the AI bubble bursts” you mean a **sharp, sustained repricing** of AI-related assets — roughly a **50%+ drawdown in leading AI names/private valuations plus a real pullback in funding and sentiment** — my **single best estimate** for that happening **within five years** is:

**45%**

Why that high: current AI valuations and capex still look ahead of proven monetization, competition is fierce, margins may get compressed, and historically many genuinely transformative technologies still went through a brutal first-wave bubble burst.

Why not higher: unlike a pure speculative mania, AI already has real demand, real revenue, powerful strategic backers, geopolitical support, and falling costs that keep expanding the use cases.

So my bottom line is: **slightly less than even odds of a true burst, but clearly high enough to take seriously.**  
Also, for an Anthropic bet specifically, your outcome depends not just on whether the bubble holds, but on **entry valuation, dilution, and whether Anthropic captures the value if AGI arrives.**

rows.answer:
Without live market data, my structural one-number estimate is **45%**.

I’m interpreting “AI bubble burst” as a **sharp, sustained repricing** of AI-related public and private valuations—roughly a **50%+ de-rating plus a real pullback in funding/capex**—not just ordinary tech volatility.

Why the probability is meaningful:
- **Valuations embed aggressive assumptions** about AGI timing, durable moats, and future margins, especially for frontier labs.
- **Capex is huge relative to proven monetization**. If enterprise ROI disappoints, today’s buildout can start to look like overinvestment.
- **Model-layer economics may commoditize**: open-source pressure, falling inference costs, and distribution power at the cloud/platform layer can squeeze vendors like Anthropic.
- A lot of AI’s economic value may go to **customers, cloud providers, or application owners**, not necessarily the frontier model companies themselves.
- **Regulation, safety incidents, IP/copyright rulings, or a recession** could reset sentiment quickly.

Why I’m not higher than that:
- The underlying technology is **real and already useful**, unlike many classic bubbles.
- The biggest spenders are **cash-rich incumbents**, not flimsy startups reliant on public markets for survival.
- General-purpose technologies often look overhyped early, but **earnings can catch up** over time.
- Strategic competition can keep investment elevated **longer than skeptics expect**.

So my best estimate is **45%**.

One extra point: “Anthropic reaches AGI first” and “Anthropic equity does well from today’s valuation” are **separate bets**. Even a real winner can be a mediocre investment if the sector gets re-priced, value accrues elsewhere, or later capital raises dilute you.

rows.answer:
Using information available through mid-2024, and defining “burst” as a sharp, lasting repricing of AI valuations plus a funding pullback, my single best estimate is:

**60%**

Why I put it above even odds:
- **Valuations and capex are extremely aggressive.** A lot of current pricing assumes fast capability gains, durable moats, and very large near-term monetization.
- **Frontier-model economics are still unclear.** Training and inference are expensive, competition is intense, and some of the value may get competed away or captured by platforms rather than labs.
- **Five years is a long window.** It’s enough time for a recession, rate shock, regulatory hit, or simple disappointment in ROI to trigger a major reset.
- **History matters.** Transformative technologies often go through bubbles and crashes before becoming genuinely world-changing industries.

Why I don’t put it much higher:
- **AI is producing real value already.** This is not pure vapor; there is real demand, real revenue, and real productivity gain.
- **The biggest backers are unusually strong.** Hyperscalers and governments can keep spending longer than typical bubble participants.
- **A “messy deflation” is possible instead of a classic crash.** The sector could grow into some of the hype, even if returns disappoint.

So my one-number answer is **60%**: a meaningful AI-bubble burst within five years is **slightly more likely than not**, even though AI itself may still turn out to be enormously important.

One important caveat: even if Anthropic reaches AGI first, that does **not** automatically mean a great investment return for you; entry price, dilution, and deal terms matter a lot.

rows.answer:
If by “AI bubble bursts” you mean a broad, sustained repricing — roughly, leading AI-linked assets falling 50%+ from peak and private AI funding/valuations staying meaningfully weaker for a year or more — my best estimate over the next five years is **55%**.

Why I put it above 50%:
- Current AI valuations still embed very optimistic assumptions about capability progress, monetization, and margins.
- The sector is extremely capital-intensive; data center, chip, and power buildouts can overshoot real demand.
- Frontier-model economics may get compressed by open-source models, price competition, and hyperscalers capturing more of the value.
- Over a five-year window, the odds are fairly high of some trigger: macro slowdown, higher rates, regulation, lawsuits, safety backlash, or a visible slowdown in model progress.
- Historically, transformative technologies often still have investment bubbles that burst before the long-run winners emerge.

Why I don’t put it much higher:
- AI already has real utility and real revenue; this is not pure vapor.
- The biggest spenders are cash-rich hyperscalers and governments, not just speculative retail capital.
- If capabilities keep improving fast enough, fundamentals can grow into today’s valuations.

So my single best estimate is: **55%**.

One extra caution: even if Anthropic reaches AGI first, that does **not** automatically mean Anthropic equity is the best-performing AI asset; bubble timing, deal terms, dilution, and who captures the value all matter.

**Single best-estimate probability: 45%.**

I’m interpreting “AI bubble burst” as a **broad, sustained repricing**—roughly a **50%+ drop across speculative AI assets plus a meaningful tightening in private funding**—not just a normal correction.

- **Many AI businesses may have weak moats.** If models commoditize or incumbents bundle features, valuations can reset fast.
- **Five years is a long window.** A recession, higher-for-longer rates, regulation, a safety incident, or a capability plateau could puncture the narrative.

Why it’s not higher:
- **AI already has real utility and revenue**, unlike many past bubbles at a similar stage.
- **The core spenders are cash-rich giants**, not fragile startups depending entirely on easy money.
- **Geopolitical/strategic importance** makes funding for frontier labs likelier to persist even in a downturn.

So: **45%** for a true “burst.”  
If you mean **any major correction**, the probability is much higher.

Why it’s above even odds: current expectations and infrastructure spending are very large relative to proven AI cash flows; moats and long-run margins for frontier model companies are still unclear; competition, open-source pressure, regulation, or a macro downturn could all trigger a repricing. Why it’s not higher: AI already has real utility and revenue, the main spenders are unusually deep-pocketed strategic players, costs are falling, and a real capability jump could justify a lot of today’s optimism.

So: more likely than not. I would not make an Anthropic thesis that depends on the bubble staying intact.

rows.answer:
Best estimate: 55%.

By “bubble burst,” I mean a sharp, sustained AI-specific repricing: major AI-related public names down roughly 40%+ and private AI valuations/funding resetting hard, not just a normal correction.

Why I put it above 50%:
- Expectations and capex are very high relative to proven ROI.
- The model layer still has shaky economics: huge compute costs, pricing pressure, and commoditization/open-source risk.
- Enterprise adoption is real, but a lot of it is still pilot-stage and slower to monetize than the hype suggests.
- Five years is a long enough window for at least one macro shock, rate shock, or recession to hit a crowded trade.
- Historically, transformative technologies often create real value and still go through an investment bust before fundamentals catch up.

Why I don’t put it much higher:
- AI already has obvious real utility; this is not pure vapor.
- The main funders are cash-rich hyperscalers and governments, which makes the ecosystem less fragile than a typical startup-only bubble.
- Even if standalone model companies get repriced, AI infrastructure and application demand may keep the broader theme from fully collapsing.

So my single-number view is: 55%.

One extra point: for Anthropic, “reaches AGI first” and “captures durable investment value before a market reset” are separate bets. Even if Anthropic wins technically, the valuation outcome can still disappoint.

rows.answer:
If I had to put one number on it, **45%**.

That assumes “the AI bubble bursts” means a **clear, broad repricing** of AI-linked assets over the next five years—roughly, a major drawdown in public AI names and a sharp contraction in private AI funding, not just a normal correction.

Why I’d put it that high:
- **Valuations already discount very strong outcomes.**
- **Capex is running ahead of proven monetization** in many parts of the stack.
- **Frontier-model economics are still uncertain** and could be squeezed by competition, open source, or commoditization.
- A **recession, tighter capital markets, regulation, or a slowdown in model progress** could easily trigger a sentiment break.

Why I wouldn’t put it higher:
- Unlike a classic pure-speculation bubble, **a lot of AI spending is being driven by cash-rich incumbents**.
- **Demand is real**: AI is already generating meaningful revenue and productivity gains.
- Some bubbles don’t “burst” suddenly; they **deflate through time** as earnings catch up.

So my single best estimate is: **45%**.

One extra note: **“Anthropic reaches AGI first” and “Anthropic equity delivers the best investment return” are not the same bet.**

**Best estimate: 45%.**

If by “AI bubble burst” you mean a sharp AI-specific repricing — roughly halved valuations for AI-heavy names plus a meaningful pullback in funding/spending — I’d put the odds within five years at **45%**.

Why it’s that high:
- Valuations and capex are running ahead of clearly proven long-term profits.
- The market may be overpricing short AGI timelines and underpricing technical slowdowns.
- Open-source models and competition could compress margins even if capabilities keep improving.
- A recession, high-rate environment, regulation, IP rulings, or a safety incident could crack sentiment.
- Real technologies still have bubbles; “AI works” and “AI is overpriced” can both be true.

Why it’s not higher:
- AI already has real utility and real revenue, unlike many past fads.
- Big Tech and governments have strategic reasons to keep funding AI through setbacks.
- The likely failure mode may be a long deflation of expectations, not a clean “pop.”

So: **material risk, but not my base case**. And for an Anthropic bet specifically, note that “they reach AGI first” and “the market keeps rewarding AI valuations” are two separate bets.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 60%.**

I’m interpreting “the AI bubble bursts” as a **serious sector-wide valuation reset and funding pullback**, not “AI turns out useless.” On that definition, I’d put the odds at **about 60% within five years**.

Why above 50%:
- **Valuations and capex are ahead of proven profits.** A lot of AI pricing assumes very large future cash flows that haven’t been demonstrated yet.
- **Value capture is unclear.** Even if AI is huge, profits may accrue more to clouds, chips, and distribution than to frontier-model labs like Anthropic.
- **Transformative tech often still has bubbles.** Railroads, telecom, and the internet were all real—and still experienced major busts.
- **Macro/regulatory risk is real.** Higher rates, recession, legal setbacks, or slower capability progress could trigger a sharp repricing.

Why not higher:
- **AI demand is already real.** This is not pure vapor; there are genuine users, revenue, and productivity gains.
- **The main spenders are very strong balance sheets.** Hyperscalers can keep funding AI longer than fragile startups could in past bubbles.
- **Costs keep falling.** Cheaper inference/training can rescue demand and make economics look better over time.
- **Governments see AI as strategic.** That creates a floor under investment.

So my base case is: **AI is likely real and important, but there’s a better-than-even chance the market gets ahead of itself and then resets hard before the winners are clear.**

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 55%**

I’d define “the AI bubble bursts” as a **clear, sector-wide valuation reset with much tighter funding**, not just a normal correction.

Why I put it **above 50%**:
- **Expectations still exceed proven cash flows.** A lot of AI pricing assumes very large future profits, while current monetization is still catching up.
- **Frontier-model economics are uncertain.** Training/inference costs are huge, competition is intense, and open-source or commoditization could compress margins.
- **Capex is running hot.** If the data-center/buildout cycle gets ahead of actual end demand, markets can reprice hard.
- **A macro, regulatory, or technical disappointment could be enough.** Higher rates, recession, copyright/safety rules, or slower-than-hoped capability gains could puncture sentiment.
- **Historically, transformative technologies often still have bubbles.** “Real technology” and “bubble that later bursts” are not mutually exclusive.

Why I don’t put it much higher:
- **AI already has real use and real revenue.** This is not purely vapor.
- **The main backers are unusually strong.** Big tech firms and governments can keep funding flowing longer than fragile startup ecosystems can.
- **Continued capability gains could validate a lot of current enthusiasm** before sentiment breaks.

So my net view is: **slightly more likely than not — 55% — that the AI bubble bursts within five years.**

One extra point: for an **Anthropic** investment, bubble risk is only part of the story. Even if the bubble doesn’t burst, your return can still disappoint if the entry valuation already prices in a huge share of AGI success.

rows.answer:
Based on information through mid-2024, my single best estimate is **45%**.

I’m interpreting “the AI bubble bursts” as a sharp, sustained repricing of AI-related valuations, plus a real pullback in funding and enthusiasm.

Why that probability is meaningfully high:
- Current valuations, especially for frontier labs and AI infrastructure, assume very large future profits and continued rapid capability gains.
- Monetization is real, but still lags the scale of spending; a lot of enterprise demand is promising rather than fully proven.
- Frontier-model businesses are expensive to run, pricing power is uncertain, and open-source plus hyperscaler control of distribution could compress margins.
- A macro shock, higher-for-longer rates, or a regulatory/safety event could puncture sentiment quickly.
- Historically, transformative technologies often create huge real value and still go through painful investor busts.

Why it isn’t higher:
- AI already has clear utility and real revenue; this is not pure story-stock speculation.
- The biggest spenders are cash-rich incumbents and strategic buyers, not mainly fragile startups or retail leverage.
- Governments increasingly see advanced AI as strategic infrastructure, which supports ongoing investment.
- If capability progress stays strong, revenues may grow into a lot of today’s optimism instead of forcing a collapse.

So, netting those out: **45%**.

One caveat: the chance that an **Anthropic** investment disappoints is higher than the chance of a broad AI-bubble burst, because private-company dilution, liquidation preferences, liquidity limits, and winner-take-most dynamics can hurt you even if the overall AI boom doesn’t crack.

rows.answer:
If by “AI bubble burst” you mean a broad, sustained repricing of AI-related valuations — say, roughly 50%+ down in major AI-linked public/private valuations plus a real funding pullback — my single best-estimate probability over the next five years is **55%**.

Why it’s above 50%:
- Current expectations embed very aggressive assumptions about capability progress, monetization, and eventual profit capture.
- AI is unusually capital-intensive, so if returns disappoint, the re-rating could be sharp.
- Frontier model providers may have weaker moats and lower margins than bulls expect if competition, open source, or cloud/platform power compresses profits.
- Five years is long enough that a macro shock, regulation, or a “progress is real but slower/less lucrative than priced” reset is fairly likely.

Why it’s not much higher:
- Unlike a pure speculative mania, AI already has real revenue, real adoption, and clear utility.
- The sector has deep-pocketed strategic backers in hyperscalers and governments, which makes a total collapse less likely.
- Even if many AI valuations fall hard, the underlying technology could still keep advancing and creating value.

So my base case is: **more likely than not, there’s a serious shakeout within five years — but not the end of AI**.

One extra note: an Anthropic investment is not a pure “first to AGI” bet; dilution, liquidation preferences, governance, and liquidity terms matter a lot.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 45%.**

That assumes “the AI bubble bursts” means a lasting, expectations-driven reset in AI-linked valuations, not just ordinary market volatility.

Why it’s not higher:
- AI already has real commercial use and growing revenue.
- The main AI leaders are cash-rich megacaps, not mostly fragile startups.
- Governments and enterprises are likely to keep funding AI even through setbacks.

Why it’s not lower:
- Current expectations still rely on very optimistic assumptions about monetization and AGI progress.
- AI capex is enormous relative to what’s been proven economically so far.
- Competition, open-source models, regulation, and infrastructure constraints could compress returns.
- Five years is a long enough window for a classic hype-cycle disappointment.

So my single best estimate is **45%**: slightly less than a coin flip.

One extra point: if your thesis is specifically “Meta will be first to AGI,” the bigger risk may be that Meta is *not* the eventual AGI winner, even if the AI bubble itself doesn’t burst. Meta is somewhat less exposed to a pure AI bubble pop than more concentrated AI names.

rows.answer:
Without live market data, I’d treat this as a structural estimate rather than a real-time forecast.

If by “the AI bubble bursts” you mean a broad, sharp repricing of AI-linked public and private valuations—roughly a 40%+ reset driven by monetization and ROI falling short of expectations—my single best estimate is:

**55% within the next five years.**

Why it’s above 50%:
- Current expectations already price in very large future profits.
- AI capex is huge and front-loaded; revenue payoff is less certain and may take longer.
- Competition and open-source could commoditize parts of the stack, hurting margins.
- Over a 5-year window, macro shocks, higher rates, regulation, or legal setbacks have plenty of time to trigger a reset.

Why it’s not higher:
- AI is already delivering real value, so this is not pure vapor.
- The biggest AI spenders are cash-rich incumbents, not fragile startups.
- Governments and large enterprises may keep funding AI even through disappointing near-term economics.

So my best number is **55%**.

One important nuance: even if the AI bubble bursts, that would not mean AI is a dead end—or that Meta necessarily loses. It would more likely mean prices got ahead of cash flows.

So: meaningful risk, but not my base case. Also, for a Meta bet, the shakier assumption may be “Meta will capture AGI economics” more than “AI enthusiasm survives five years intact.”

rows.answer:
If by “AI bubble bursts” you mean a **dot-com-style expectation reset**—roughly, major AI-linked stocks down 40%+, private AI valuations/funding falling hard, and the narrative shifting from “AGI-driven windfall soon” to “returns are much farther off than priced in”—my **best estimate is 55% within five years**.

Why I lean slightly above even odds:

- **Valuations and capex are running ahead of proven monetization.** A lot of spending is justified by very large future profit pools that are still uncertain.
- **Transformative technologies often do have bubbles that burst** before the underlying tech fully wins. Railroads, telecom, and the internet all fit that pattern.
- **A slowdown in model progress, weak enterprise ROI, regulation, power/compute constraints, or higher rates** could trigger a repricing.

Why it’s not much higher:

- **AI is not pure vapor.** There is already real revenue, real adoption, and real productivity value.
- **The leaders are mostly cash-rich incumbents** rather than fragile startups, which makes a full collapse less likely than in classic bubbles.
- Even **without AGI**, AI can still support meaningful earnings growth in software, ads, cloud, chips, and automation.

So my single-number answer is: **55%**.

That also means: even if AI turns out to be genuinely huge, there is still a better-than-even chance that the *financial trade* around AI gets ahead of itself and resets before the long-term payoff arrives.

rows.answer:
If by “AI bubble burst” you mean a **sustained ~40%+ repricing in AI-linked public/private valuations plus a real pullback in funding/capex**, my **single best-estimate probability is 55% within the next five years**.

Why it’s that high:
- **Expectations are very elevated** and often embed huge AGI-era profits.
- **AI capex is running ahead of clearly proven monetization** in a lot of use cases.
- **Transformative technologies often get overbuilt and then repriced** before the long-term winners emerge.
- A normal **recession, rate shock, regulation, or model-economics disappointment** could puncture sentiment.

Why it’s not higher:
- **The underlying technology is real and useful already**, unlike many pure-speculation bubbles.
- The biggest AI spenders are mostly **cash-rich incumbents** rather than weak companies dependent on easy financing.
- **Geopolitical competition** and productivity gains make AI spending more durable than a typical hype cycle.

So my bottom line is: **55%**.

One extra nuance: even if that happens, it would not necessarily mean AI is “fake” or that **Meta** does badly—just that market expectations got ahead of business reality.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 55%.**

I’m defining “the AI bubble bursts” as a **broad, sustained ~40%+ repricing in AI-linked stocks/private valuations driven mainly by AI-specific disappointment**, not just ordinary market volatility.

Why I lean slightly above 50%:
- **Valuations and capex are pricing in very large profit pools** and fairly fast capability gains.
- There’s real **overbuild risk** in GPUs, data centers, and power if monetization lags.
- **Enterprise adoption is real but can be slower than demos suggest** because of integration, governance, and reliability issues.
- The **model and app layers may commoditize**, which could shrink margins even if usage explodes.
- **Rates, recession, regulation, copyright/safety setbacks, or a major public failure** could trigger a sharp narrative reversal.

Why I don’t put it much higher:
- AI already has **real revenue and real utility**; this isn’t purely vapor.
- The biggest AI players are **cash-rich mega-caps**, not fragile zero-revenue firms.
- **Government and strategic spending** can keep the buildout going even through weak short-term ROI.
- A genuine **capability leap** could validate a lot of today’s optimism.

So my single-number forecast is **55%**: a serious AI valuation washout in the next five years is **slightly more likely than not**.

One extra point: **the technology can win while the trade loses**. Even if AI keeps advancing—and even if Meta turns out to be a major winner—the market could still re-rate AI spending hard before the payoff shows up.

rows.answer:
Not investment advice, but my single best estimate is **45%**.

I’m interpreting “the AI bubble bursts” as a **broad AI-specific repricing** within five years: major AI-linked public names down roughly **50%+** from peak, with private AI funding also tightening hard for a sustained period.

Why I put it that high:

- **Capex is huge and front-loaded.** Data center, chip, and model spending is being committed before the long-run ROI is fully proven.
- **AI may commoditize faster than investors expect.** If models become interchangeable, margins could compress and the profit pool could be smaller than the market assumes.
- **Five years is plenty of time for a trigger.** Recession, higher-for-longer rates, regulation, copyright setbacks, or a capabilities plateau could all puncture sentiment.

Why I don’t put it higher:
- **The technology is real and already useful.** This is not pure vapor; AI is already improving coding, ads, search, support, and workflow automation.
- **The biggest AI spenders are cash-rich incumbents.** That makes the setup less fragile than the dot-com era.
- **Even without AGI, narrower AI can still create real earnings.** That can keep the theme alive even if the hype cools.

So my bottom line is **45%**: a true bust is **slightly less likely than not, but very plausible**.

One nuance: that’s the probability of the **AI theme** suffering a real bust, not the probability that **Meta** specifically collapses. Meta’s core ad business gives it more cushion than a pure-play AI name.

rows.answer:
If by “AI bubble burst” you mean a sharp, sustained repricing of AI-related assets and spending—roughly, major AI-linked stocks down 40%+ and a clear pullback in AI funding/capex lasting a year or more—my single best estimate is:

**45% within the next five years.**

Why it’s that high:
- Current expectations in many AI names imply very fast monetization and possibly AGI-like progress.
- AI infrastructure spending is enormous relative to clearly proven near-term ROI.
- Competition and open-source models could compress margins.
- A recession, higher real rates, regulation, or a technical progress slowdown could puncture sentiment.

Why it’s not higher:
- Unlike a pure story bubble, AI already has real usage, revenue, and strategic value.
- The biggest spenders are cash-rich incumbents, not just speculative startups.
- Governments and large enterprises are likely to keep investing even through disappointment.
- The more likely downside may be a shakeout or rotation rather than a total collapse.

So my best estimate is **45%**.  
Also: even if the AI bubble bursts, that would not necessarily mean AI is over or that Meta can’t still win; it would mainly mean valuations/spending got ahead of realized economics.

rows.answer:
**45%**

I’m interpreting “the AI bubble bursts” as a broad, sustained repricing of AI-linked assets—something like a major drop in AI-driven valuations that lasts, not just a brief correction.

Why that risk is real:
- **Expectations are very high.** Parts of the market are pricing in fast monetization, strong moat durability, and in some cases AGI-adjacent outcomes.
- **Capex is enormous.** Hyperscalers are spending at a pace that may outrun near-term returns, which is classic bubble fuel.
- **History is not reassuring.** Even genuinely transformative technologies often go through bubbles that burst before the long-term value becomes clear.
- **There are several plausible triggers.** Slower model progress, weak enterprise ROI, regulation, copyright issues, recession, or higher-for-longer rates could all reset sentiment.

Why I wouldn’t put the odds higher:
- **The leaders are real businesses.** Unlike dot-com-era favorites, Meta, Microsoft, Alphabet, Amazon, and Nvidia have large cash flows and can fund AI internally.
- **AI already has real uses.** Even without AGI, it is improving ads, coding, search, customer support, and cloud demand.
- **Strategic spending may stay strong.** Governments and large firms have incentives to keep investing even if short-term returns are mixed.

So my single best estimate is **45%**: a serious AI-bubble break within five years is quite plausible, but still a bit less likely than not.

One important nuance: a bubble can burst **even if AI ultimately succeeds**. And for Meta specifically, the froth risk is lower than for pure-play AI names because much of its value still comes from its core ad business.

I don’t have live 2026 market data, so using a base-rate/structural view:

**Best estimate: 60%**

That assumes **“AI bubble bursts”** means a **broad 50%+ repricing in AI-linked valuations** and a clear cooling in funding/sentiment within the next five years.

Why that’s **above 50%**:
- Big transformative-tech booms usually **overshoot before fundamentals catch up**.
- Current AI enthusiasm embeds very aggressive assumptions about **AGI timelines, monetization, and ROI on huge capex**.
- Over five years, the odds are meaningful that some combination of **recession, higher rates, regulation, lawsuits, energy constraints, or a technical plateau** triggers a reset.

Why it’s **not higher**:
- Unlike the dot-com bubble, the main public AI winners are mostly **cash-rich, profitable megacaps**.
- AI already has **real utility and real revenue**, so this isn’t pure vapor.
- A genuine breakthrough could let fundamentals **grow into** today’s expectations.

So my single-number answer is: **60%**.

One nuance: even if the AI bubble bursts, that does **not** necessarily mean Meta is a bad investment—Meta is less of a pure AI proxy than many other AI-linked assets.

Using “burst” to mean a broad, sustained repricing of AI-linked valuations and funding—not just a normal correction—my **single best estimate is 45%** over the next five years.

Why it’s that high:
- Expectations around near-term AGI and AI monetization still look aggressive.
- AI capex is enormous relative to clearly proven end-user ROI.
- Transformative technologies often create real value **and** still go through a financial bubble/bust cycle.
- Competition, regulation, power constraints, or a recession/rate shock could puncture sentiment.

Why it’s not higher:
- AI already has real demand, real revenue, and some genuine productivity gains.
- The biggest spenders are cash-rich megacaps and governments, not just fragile startups.
- Even if AGI timelines slip, narrower AI products and infrastructure can keep compounding.

So my bottom line is: **45%**.  
Slightly less than a coin flip that the AI trade sees a serious, narrative-changing reset within five years.

One Meta-specific note: the harder leg of your thesis is probably not “the AI bubble stays intact,” but “Meta captures an outsized share of AGI value.”

rows.answer:
Best estimate: **45%**

Assuming “burst” means a **clear, broad, lasting re-rating of AI-linked valuations**, not just a normal tech pullback, I’d put the odds at about **45% over the next five years**.

Why the odds are that high:
- **Expectations are extremely aggressive.** A lot of pricing assumes rapid progress, durable pricing power, and very large profits from AI.
- **Capex is real now; monetization is less certain.** Companies are spending heavily on chips, data centers, and talent before it’s clear the revenue will justify it.
- **Competition could crush margins.** If models become more interchangeable, or open-source keeps improving, value may shift away from model makers.
- **Macro can puncture narratives.** Higher rates, a recession, or tighter regulation could force investors to care more about near-term cash flow.

Why the odds aren’t higher:
- **AI is a real technology with real adoption.** This is not pure vapor; there are genuine productivity gains and commercial use cases already.
- **The core players are unusually strong.** Unlike past bubbles, many of the main spenders are mega-cap firms with huge cash flows and the ability to keep investing through downturns.
- **Strategic and geopolitical incentives matter.** Governments and large platforms may keep funding AI even if short-term returns disappoint.

So my single-number call is **45%**: a serious risk, but not the most likely outcome.

One extra nuance: for **Meta specifically**, the risk is probably **lower than for the AI sector overall**, because Meta also has a large non-AI business and can benefit from AI without literally being first to AGI.

rows.answer:
If by “the AI bubble bursts” you mean a clear, sustained valuation reset in AI-linked assets — not just normal tech volatility — my **single best estimate is 45% over the next five years**.

Why it’s not higher:
- AI already has real economic value; this isn’t pure story-stock territory.
- The main spenders are cash-rich giants like Meta, Microsoft, Alphabet, Amazon, and Nvidia, not fragile companies dependent on easy financing.
- Governments and enterprises increasingly treat AI as strategic infrastructure, which supports continued spending.

Why it’s not lower:
- Expectations around AGI timelines, margins, and monetization are extremely ambitious.
- AI capex is enormous relative to proven ROI in many use cases; a disappointment cycle is very plausible.
- Competition and open-source progress could commoditize models, so even if AI keeps improving, profits may disappoint.
- A recession, higher-for-longer rates, regulation, or a visible model-progress slowdown could trigger a sharp repricing.

The key point: a burst would most likely mean **valuations ran ahead of monetizable economics**, not that AI “was fake.” That can happen even if Meta remains a long-term winner.

So: **45%**.

- **AI is not fake demand.** Unlike many classic bubbles, there is already real usage, real spending, and real productivity benefit.
- **The leaders are strong incumbents.** Meta, Microsoft, Alphabet, Amazon, Nvidia, etc. are profitable and can fund the buildout from existing businesses.
- **Spending likely continues even after disappointment.** A bubble can burst without AI itself going away.

Most likely failure mode: **not “AI was nonsense,” but “AI was real, just overvalued and overpromised in the short run.”**

For **Meta specifically**, a burst would probably hurt less than it would hurt pure-play AI names, because Meta still has a very strong core ads business. But if your thesis requires **both** “Meta reaches AGI first” **and** “no major AI repricing for years,” that’s a fairly demanding bet.

*Forecast, not investment advice.*

rows.answer:
Assuming **“the AI bubble bursts”** means a **broad, sharp market re-rating of AI-linked assets**—not “AI turns out to be useless” but something like a major sentiment reset and big multiple compression—my **single best estimate is 55%** over the next five years.

Why slightly above even odds:
- **Historical base rate:** transformative technologies often create real value *and* still go through bubbles that pop.
- **Capex vs. payoff risk:** AI spending is massive, while durable monetization and margins are still uncertain.
- **Crowded positioning:** a lot of value is concentrated in a few names and narratives, which makes the theme vulnerable to a macro shock, rate shock, or earnings disappointment.

Why not higher:
- **AI is already genuinely useful**, so this isn’t pure vapor.
- **The biggest players are cash-rich incumbents** like Meta, Microsoft, Alphabet, etc., not fragile startups dependent on cheap financing.
- There’s a decent chance the bubble **deflates through earnings catching up**, rather than bursting outright.

So: **55%**.  
And importantly, **AI can be real and still have its bubble burst**—those are not contradictory outcomes.

rows.answer:
Using conditions visible through mid-2024, and defining “AI bubble bursts” as a broad, sustained repricing in AI-linked assets — say, leading AI stocks down ~40%+ from peak along with a real funding/sentiment unwind — my single best estimate for the next five years is **55%**.

Why I put it above 50:
- Expectations and capex are running ahead of clearly proven end-market profits.
- AGI timelines are especially vulnerable to disappointment.
- Competition and open-source pressure could erode margins even if usage keeps growing.
- A 5-year window is long enough for a recession, higher-for-longer rates, or regulation/litigation to trigger a sharp repricing.
- Historically, even genuinely transformative technologies often go through bubbles that burst.

Why it’s not higher:
- AI is already delivering real utility and real revenue.
- The main spenders are cash-rich megacaps, not mostly fragile startups.
- Governments and large enterprises are likely to keep AI strategically important.

So my best estimate is **55%**. Also, that’s a sector-bubble estimate, not a Meta-specific one; Meta is less exposed than a pure-play AI stock because its core ad business still matters a lot.

rows.answer:
If I have to put one number on it: **45%**.

I’m interpreting “the AI bubble bursts” as **a clear AI-specific valuation reset**—roughly a **40%+ drop in leading AI-linked valuations**, plus a noticeable cooling in funding and capex.

Why I put it that high:
- **Expectations are very aggressive.** A lot of pricing still assumes rapid monetization and, implicitly, fairly short timelines to much more powerful AI.
- **Capex is huge.** Big buildouts in chips, data centers, and power can overshoot near-term demand; historically that often ends in a correction.
- **Monetization is still uncertain.** AI usage is real, but durable margins and willingness to pay are less proven than the hype implies.
- **Competition may compress profits.** Open-source models and fast-following rivals could make AI less winner-take-all than bulls expect.
- **Macro/regulatory shocks could be the catalyst.** Recession, higher rates, litigation, or regulation could puncture sentiment.

Why I don’t put it above 50%:
- **The core AI spenders are cash-rich incumbents.** This is not as fragile as a bubble driven mainly by unprofitable startups.
- **There is real underlying value already.** AI is improving ads, coding, search, customer service, and cloud demand today.
- **A correction is not the same as a burst.** Earnings may partially grow into valuations before a full narrative collapse happens.

So my single best-estimate probability is: **45%**.

One caveat: even if an AI bubble bursts, **Meta may hold up better than pure-play AI names** because its ad business is still the core engine. But “Meta is first to AGI” is a much narrower bet than “the AI bubble survives.”

rows.answer:
If by **“AI bubble bursts”** you mean **a broad, AI-driven repricing in public markets**—roughly **40%+ down in AI-linked stocks, sustained for a year or more, because AI monetization/AGI expectations get marked down**—my **single best estimate is 45% within the next five years**.

Why it’s that high:
- **Hype-cycle base rates**: transformative tech often goes through a sharp disillusionment phase even when the tech is real.
- **Expectations are aggressive**: current spending and valuations imply strong assumptions about AGI timing, pricing power, and returns on huge capex.
- **Plausible triggers**: slower model progress, weak enterprise ROI, model commoditization, regulation, or a macro/rates shock.

Why it’s not higher:
- The main AI leaders are **huge, profitable incumbents**, not fragile pre-revenue startups.
- **Real demand already exists** for AI tools and infrastructure, even short of AGI.
- Strategic spending by governments and hyperscalers can **extend the cycle** and soften a bust.

So: **45%**.  
Also, **Meta is less exposed than pure AI names**, because its core ads business can still support it even if the AI trade cools.

rows.answer:
Rough judgment, not personal financial advice:

If by “the AI bubble bursts” you mean a sustained, narrative-breaking repricing in AI-linked stocks and private funding — not just a normal tech correction — my best estimate is **45%** within the next five years.

Why it’s that high:
- Current expectations in parts of AI still price in very large future profits that may take longer to arrive.
- AI capex is enormous, and the return on that spending is not yet fully proven.
- Model performance may keep improving while economics get worse because competition and commoditization squeeze margins.
- Regulation, copyright, energy, and macro shocks could all puncture sentiment.

Why it’s not higher:
- AI already has real users, real revenue, and real productivity value.
- The biggest spenders are cash-rich megacaps and governments, not mostly fragile startups.
- Even without AGI, AI can materially improve ads, search, coding, and enterprise workflows.
- Falling inference and tooling costs should keep adoption growing.

rows.answer:
If by “AI bubble burst” you mean a broad, narrative-breaking repricing of AI-linked assets—not just a normal correction—my single best estimate is **55%** over the next five years.

Why it’s above 50%:
- Current pricing reflects very aggressive assumptions about AI monetization and possibly AGI timing.
- Capex is enormous, and the payoff is still uncertain for many use cases.
- Model performance may improve more slowly than hype implies, while competition and open-source could compress margins.
- Five years is a long enough window for a macro shock, regulation, or a sentiment reversal.

Why it’s not higher:
- Unlike classic bubbles, the main AI leaders are highly profitable incumbents with real cash flow.
- AI already has genuine utility, so even a disappointment cycle may look more like a sharp repricing than a full collapse.
- Real adoption could catch up to expectations over that time horizon.

So: **55%**.  
Also, “Google gets to AGI first” and “the AI bubble doesn’t burst” are related, but not the same bet.

rows.answer:
**Single best-estimate probability: 45%.**

That assumes “the AI bubble bursts” means a **sustained, sentiment-driven 40%+ de-rating** in AI-linked valuations, not just a normal tech correction.

Why I put it that high:
- **Expectations are very aggressive.** Current pricing assumes large, durable AI profit pools and fairly fast progress.
- **Capex is huge; monetization is real but still not fully proven.** If returns lag spending, sentiment can flip hard.
- **Competition could crush margins.** Even if AI works, model providers may not capture much of the value.
- **Five years is a long window.** A recession, rate shock, regulation, or progress slowdown is quite plausible in that time.

Why I don’t put it higher:
- **AI is already useful.** This is not pure vapor; there is real adoption, revenue, and measurable ROI.
- **The key buyers are cash-rich incumbents.** That makes a classic forced unwind less likely than in past bubbles.
- **Governments and enterprises increasingly see AI as strategic infrastructure,** which supports demand even if hype cools.

So my bottom line is: **slightly less than a coin flip, but close.**

Also: **“Google gets to AGI first” and “Google stock wins” are not the same bet.** Monetization, search cannibalization, competition, and regulation could matter more than who is “first.”

rows.answer:
**Single best estimate: 45%.**

That’s my estimate for a **real AI-bubble burst** within five years: a broad, sustained repricing of AI-linked valuations and a meaningful pullback in funding/sentiment, not just an ordinary correction.

Why I put it that high:
- Expectations are very aggressive and often implicitly price in near-term AGI or huge profit pools.
- AI capex is enormous relative to clearly proven monetization so far.
- A slowdown in model progress, weaker enterprise ROI, regulation/litigation, or a macro downturn could puncture the story.

Why I don’t put it higher:
- AI already has real utility and some real revenue, unlike a purely story-driven mania.
- The biggest AI spenders are cash-rich incumbents, not fragile startups.
- Governments and major enterprises have strategic reasons to keep investing even through disappointment.

So: **45%**.

One extra point: Alphabet is **not** a pure “AI bubble” bet. Even if the AI bubble bursts, Google could still hold up better than a pure-play AI name because of Search, Cloud, and its balance sheet. And even if Google reaches AGI first, shareholder payoff still depends on commercialization and competition, not just being first.

As a rough forecasting estimate, assuming “AI bubble bursts” means a major AI-specific repricing — not just a normal bear market, but a clear sentiment break driven by weaker-than-expected AI economics or progress — my best estimate is **55%** over the next five years.

Why around there:
- **For a burst:** expectations are very high, AGI timelines are highly uncertain, and the capex being poured into AI may outrun near-term returns. Historically, even real transformative technologies often go through a boom-bust phase before the long-run value is realized.
- **Against a burst:** AI already has real demand and real revenue, and the main players are cash-rich incumbents rather than fragile startups. That makes a full dot-com-style collapse less likely.

So if you force me to give one number: **55%**.

One extra note: “Google reaches AGI first” and “Google stock is a great bet” are not exactly the same thesis; Google is more insulated than pure AI plays, but its stock will still depend a lot on monetization, search defense, and regulation.

rows.answer:
If by “AI bubble bursts” you mean a broad, durable repricing of AI-exposed stocks and expectations — say a 35–50% drop in the main AI winners plus a clear shift from “AI will monetize fast” to “returns are farther out than hoped” — my single best estimate for the next five years is **45%**.

Why I’m not higher:
- AI already has real demand and real revenue, unlike pure vaporware manias.
- The main spenders/winners are mostly cash-rich incumbents, not fragile startups.
- Governments and large enterprises have strategic reasons to keep funding AI even through weak patches.

Why I’m not lower:
- Current expectations still imply extremely large future profits.
- Capex is running well ahead of clearly proven monetization.
- Competition and open-source models can compress margins.
- Regulation, copyright/liability, energy constraints, or a macro slowdown could easily trigger a major rerating.
- Historically, transformative technologies often do produce a real bubble-and-bust even when the technology itself is genuinely important.

So: **45%**.

One extra caveat: “Google gets to AGI first” and “Google stock is the best way to profit from AGI” are separate bets; a bubble can burst even if the underlying technology keeps advancing.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 45%**

I’m interpreting “AI bubble burst” as a real AI-specific valuation and funding reset — roughly a 40%+ drop in AI-linked equities/startup values relative to the broader market, driven by disappointment in profits or progress, not just a normal correction.

Why that probability is fairly high:
- AI spending is enormous relative to clearly proven monetization so far.
- AGI timelines are extremely uncertain, so expectations can outrun reality.
- Competition and open models could commoditize a lot of the value.
- Big general-purpose tech booms often overshoot before settling.
- A macro slowdown, higher rates, regulation, lawsuits, or a visible progress plateau could trigger a repricing.

Why it isn’t higher:
- AI already has real utility and real revenue, unlike many pure hype waves.
- The main spenders are giant, profitable incumbents with strong balance sheets.
- Even if some use cases disappoint, enterprise adoption and infrastructure demand may remain durable.
- A genuine technical breakthrough could validate a lot of current optimism before sentiment breaks.

So my single best estimate is **45%** over the next five years.

One extra nuance: Google is not a pure AI-bubble proxy. Even if the AI sector de-rates, Alphabet’s existing Search/YouTube/Cloud cash flows make it more resilient than a pure-play AI stock.

If by **“AI bubble bursts”** you mean a **broad, sharp repricing of AI-linked public and private valuations**—something like a sustained **30–50%+ drawdown** driven by disappointment in AI economics, progress, or policy—my **single best estimate** is:

**45% within the next five years.**

Why not higher:
- AI already has **real demand and real revenue**, unlike many pure hype manias.
- The biggest AI spenders are mostly **cash-rich incumbents** rather than fragile startups.
- Even if AGI takes longer, AI can still create enough productivity gains to support a lot of investment.

Why not lower:
- Current expectations embed **very aggressive assumptions** about capability gains and monetization.
- The sector is absorbing **enormous capex**; if returns look weak, sentiment can flip fast.
- AI economics may get **commoditized**, so technical progress may not translate into outsized profits.
- A macro shock, regulation, copyright rulings, or energy/compute bottlenecks could trigger a broader unwind.

For your specific Google thesis, the key nuance is: **Google does not need to be first to AGI to be a good investment, and being first to AGI would not automatically make it one.** The stock outcome depends more on **monetization, search defense, cloud positioning, and capital discipline** than on crossing an “AGI first” finish line.

But on the narrow question: **45%**.

rows.answer:
If by **“AI bubble burst”** you mean **more than a normal correction**—roughly, a **40%+ sustained repricing** in AI-linked equities, a hard reset in private AI valuations, and a meaningful pullback in AI capex—my **single best-estimate probability over the next five years is 45%**.

Why it’s that high:
- **Expectations are very elevated** relative to current AI cash flows.
- **Capex is enormous**, and the ROI case is still not fully proven.
- The biggest risk is not “AI fails”; it’s **AI works but profits get competed away** by open-source models, commoditization, and price pressure.
- Over a 5-year window, **macro shocks, higher rates, regulation, IP rulings, or safety incidents** are all plausible triggers.
- Historically, **transformative technologies often have bubbles that burst before the long-run winners fully monetize them**.

Why it’s not higher:
- AI already has **real users, real use cases, and real revenue**; this is not pure vapor.
- The main spenders are **cash-rich incumbents** like Google, Microsoft, Amazon, and Meta, not fragile companies dependent on cheap financing.
- AI is now **strategically core infrastructure**, so spending can continue even if near-term returns disappoint.
- **Alphabet specifically is diversified**; it is not a pure AI hype vehicle.

So my best estimate is **45%**: **not the most likely single outcome, but close enough to a coin flip that I’d treat an AI-bust scenario as a central risk, not a tail risk**.

One extra point: betting on Google because it might be “first to AGI” is a **different** question from whether the AI bubble bursts. Even if Google wins technically, the stock outcome will depend more on **monetization, moat, and whether AI helps or hurts Search economics** than on a headline milestone alone.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 55%.**

If by “the AI bubble bursts” you mean a **broad, sustained repricing of AI-linked valuations**—not “AI turns out useless,” but something like a major drawdown in the hottest AI names/private valuations plus a clear cooling in funding and capex—then I’d put the odds at **about 55% over the next five years**.

Why it’s that high:
- Current pricing in many AI-related assets reflects **very optimistic assumptions** about AGI timing, monetization, and margins.
- **Capex is enormous** and ROI is still not fully proven.
- New-tech manias often see a **sharp correction before fundamentals catch up**.
- A recession, higher rates, regulation, safety backlash, copyright issues, energy constraints, or slower-than-hoped model progress could trigger it.
- Competition and open-source models could **commoditize** parts of the stack.

Why it’s not higher:
- AI already has **real use cases, real revenue, and real productivity gains**.
- The main spenders are mostly **cash-rich incumbents**, not just speculative startups.
- Governments and large enterprises are likely to keep funding AI even through a downturn.
- The “bubble” could also **deflate gradually** instead of bursting suddenly.

So: **slightly more likely than not**. A real AI-valuation unwind within five years is plausible, but not inevitable. And even if it happens, that would not necessarily mean **Alphabet** itself performs badly, since it has large non-AI cash flows and multiple business lines.

I don’t have live market data, so treat this as a structural forecast, not a trading call.

**Best estimate: 45%**

Assuming “the AI bubble bursts” means a **sharp, sustained repricing of AI-linked valuations** driven by expectations outrunning realized profits, here’s how I’d weigh it:

**Why the risk is meaningful**
- AI has several classic bubble traits: narrative dominance, crowded positioning, and very large capital spending justified by very large future expectations.
- A lot depends on profits showing up fast enough. If enterprise ROI disappoints, markets can turn quickly.
- Frontier-model competition may make AI more useful while making it **less** profitable than investors expect.
- AGI timelines are highly uncertain. If markets are implicitly pricing “AGI soon,” delay alone can puncture the story.
- A macro slowdown, higher rates, regulation, power constraints, or legal setbacks could trigger a sentiment reversal.

**Why I wouldn’t put it higher**
- Unlike many past bubbles, the underlying technology is already genuinely useful.
- The main AI spenders are mostly giant, cash-rich firms, not just speculative startups.
- Even if the hype cools, AI adoption can keep growing, which makes a slower deflation more plausible than a clean collapse.
- History shows a technology can be real and still have a bubble, but real utility reduces the odds of a total air pocket.

So my single best estimate is **45%**. A burst is very possible, but not my base case.

rows.answer:
Assuming “the AI bubble bursts” means a broad, sustained repricing of AI-linked stocks and private valuations—not just a normal correction—my best estimate is **45%** over the next five years.

Why that’s meaningfully possible:
- Current expectations embed very large future profits and very fast progress.
- AI infrastructure spending is huge relative to the amount of clearly proven AI monetization so far.
- Even if AI usage explodes, competition and open-source models could compress margins.
- A macro shock, higher real rates, regulation, copyright liability, or a high-profile safety failure could trigger a sharp sentiment reversal.

Why it’s not higher:
- This boom is being funded mainly by very profitable incumbents, not fragile startups with no cash flow.
- AI already has real utility and some real revenue; it isn’t purely story-driven.
- Governments and large enterprises may keep spending for strategic reasons even if short-term ROI is messy.
- Sometimes fundamentals grow into the hype instead of the hype collapsing.

The key historical point is that a technology can be genuinely transformative and still have a bubble. So the question isn’t “Will AI matter?” It’s “Will profits arrive fast enough to justify current expectations before markets lose patience?”

My answer: **45%**.  
For Alphabet specifically, that risk is somewhat lower-impact than for a pure AI stock, because much of its value still comes from existing businesses rather than AI optionality alone.

rows.answer:
Using a mid-2024 information set, and defining an **“AI bubble burst”** as a **broad, lasting re-rating of AI-linked assets**—not just a routine correction—my **single best-estimate probability is 45% within the next five years**.

Why that probability is fairly high:
- **Capex is enormous** relative to clearly proven AI monetization.
- **Model economics may commoditize**, which would undermine some of today’s margin assumptions.
- A **recession / higher-rate shock / credit tightening** could hit a hype-heavy theme hard.
- Historically, **transformative technologies often do go through a bubble-and-bust phase** before the real long-term winners emerge.

Why it’s not higher:
- Unlike the dot-com era, the main AI spenders are **cash-rich incumbents** with real earnings.
- AI already has **real use cases and real revenue**, even if the long-run payoff is uncertain.
- Governments and large enterprises see AI as **strategic infrastructure**, which supports spending even through disappointment.

So my bottom line is: **45%**.

One extra point for your Google thesis: **even if Google reaches AGI first, the stock may not fully benefit if the market decides AI economics were overhyped or if AI pressures Google’s existing search margins.**
