rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 45%.**

That’s assuming “AI bubble bursts” means a broad, sustained repricing of AI-linked assets because expectations run ahead of realized profits—not that AI stops improving.

Why it’s not higher:
- AI already has real utility and real demand.
- The biggest AI spenders are cash-rich incumbents, not fragile startups funded only by cheap money.
- Governments and large firms increasingly treat frontier AI as strategic infrastructure.

Why it’s not lower:
- Valuations and capex still imply very aggressive future profits.
- Outside chips/cloud, monetization is less proven than the market narrative suggests.
- Model progress could slow, margins could get commoditized, or regulation/litigation/energy constraints could bite.
- A normal recession or rate shock could puncture the narrative even if the technology keeps advancing.

So my single-number view is **45%**: less than a coin flip, but high enough that “Google wins AGI first” is not, by itself, a safe investment thesis. AI can be real and still have a bubble burst.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 55%**

That’s assuming “the AI bubble bursts” means a **broad, durable repricing** in AI-linked public and private valuations — say, something like a **35–50% drawdown** in the theme plus a sharp funding pullback, not just a normal correction.

Why I’d put it slightly above 50%:

- **Expectations are very high.** A lot of AI valuations implicitly assume very fast monetization, very strong margins, and/or near-AGI-level progress on a fairly short timeline.
- **Capex is running ahead of proven returns.** The spending on chips, datacenters, power, and talent is enormous. If realized profits lag that spend, markets can reset hard.
- **History favors a shakeout.** Transformative technologies often do change the world and still have an investment bubble first. The internet is the obvious analogy.
- **Profits may be competed away.** AI can be hugely valuable for society while still being a less attractive business than current prices assume, especially if models commoditize or open-source keeps pressure on pricing.
- **Five years is long enough** for a recession, higher-for-longer rates, regulation, litigation, or a technical plateau to puncture sentiment.

Why I’m not saying 70%+:

- **This isn’t pure vapor.** AI already has real demand, real revenue, and real productivity gains.
- **The leaders are cash-rich incumbents.** That makes the boom more durable than a startup-only mania financed by cheap money.
- **Strategic demand matters.** Governments and large enterprises may keep spending even if near-term ROI is messy.

So my single-number answer is **55%**: **slightly more likely than not** that the AI boom sees a meaningful bust within the next five years.

One important subtlety: **the bubble can burst even if AI succeeds.** For a Google thesis, that means you’re really making two bets: that Google wins technically, and that the market keeps rewarding the AI story long enough for that win to show up in the stock.

rows.answer:
**Single best estimate: 55%.**

By “AI bubble burst,” I mean a broad, sustained repricing of AI-linked valuations and a meaningful pullback in funding and capex, driven by disappointment versus expectations.

Why I put it **above even odds**:
- Expectations already reflect very ambitious outcomes, sometimes implicitly including AGI-adjacent success.
- AI spending is enormous, but near-term ROI is still uneven and hard to prove in many use cases.
- Model capabilities may keep improving while economics get worse: competition, open source, and falling model prices can compress margins.
- Regulation, copyright/liability issues, power constraints, and a macro slowdown could all trigger a reset.
- Historically, general-purpose technologies often get overcapitalized before the profits fully show up.

Why I **don’t** put it much higher:
- AI already has real utility and real revenue; it is not pure vapor.
- The biggest AI spenders are cash-rich megacaps, not mostly fragile startups dependent on easy money.
- A hype cycle can cool gradually rather than “pop” in a classic crash.

So my base case is: **slightly more likely than not** that the AI bubble bursts within five years, but more as a **valuation/expectations reset** than as a verdict that AI itself was a mirage.

If your Google thesis only works in the “no bubble burst” world, I’d treat that as a fragile thesis. Google could still do fine even if AI enthusiasm re-rates downward, because it isn’t just an AI story.

rows.answer:
Using a fairly strict definition of “the AI bubble bursts” — e.g. AI-linked valuations/public stocks reset by ~40%+ and stay depressed for at least a year because monetization/adoption disappoints — my single best estimate is:

**45% within the next five years.**

Why that high:
- **Expectations are very aggressive.** A lot of value is being assigned to future AI profits that may take longer to arrive than markets hope.
- **Capex is huge relative to proven ROI.** If companies spend massively on GPUs/data centers and the revenue doesn’t materialize fast enough, sentiment can flip hard.
- **Transformative technologies often still have bubbles.** Railroads, radio, the internet, clean tech: real tech can still be a bad investment for a while if expectations outrun economics.
- **Five years is a long enough window** for recession, higher rates, regulation, lawsuits, or an “AGI is farther away than expected” narrative shift.

Why not higher:
- **This isn’t pure dot-com 1999.** The main AI players are profitable giants with real cash flow, not just story stocks.
- **AI already has real use cases.** Coding, search, ads, enterprise support, content workflows, and analytics are generating genuine value now.
- **A lot of the spend is being done by companies that can afford it.** That makes a gradual deflation more plausible than a sudden collapse.

For **Google/Alphabet specifically**, I’d add one nuance: even if there is an AI-bubble bust, Alphabet is **less exposed than a pure AI play** because you’re also buying a huge ads business, cloud, and buybacks. On the flip side, “Google reaches AGI first” is **not** a clean stock thesis by itself; being first to AGI is neither necessary nor sufficient for the stock to outperform.

So if you force me to one number: **45%**.

rows.answer:
I don’t have live market data beyond mid-2024, so this is a structural estimate, not a read on today’s exact pricing.

**Best estimate: 55%.**

That assumes “AI bubble bursts” means a broad sentiment/valuation reset in AI-linked assets—say a 30–50%+ repricing and tighter funding conditions—not just a normal correction.

Why I put it a bit above 50%:
- **Expectations are extremely high.** A lot of spending and valuation already assumes very large near-term AI profits.
- **Monetization is still catching up.** AI is clearly useful, but the realized ROI may lag the hype.
- **Transformative tech often still bubbles and bursts.** The internet was real; the dot-com bubble still burst.
- **There are several plausible pins:** recession, higher rates, regulation, lawsuits, energy/chip constraints, or slower-than-hoped model progress.

Why I don’t put it much higher:
- **Unlike dot-com, the biggest AI spenders are cash-rich incumbents** like Google, Microsoft, Meta, and Amazon.
- **AI already has real demand and real revenue**, so fundamentals may partially grow into the hype.
- **Alphabet is less fragile than pure-play AI names** because it has large existing cash flows from search, YouTube, and cloud.

So my single-number answer is **55%**.

One important caveat: **“Google is first to AGI” is not the same as “Google stock wins big.”** Even if Google gets there first, the market payoff could be muted by search cannibalization, higher compute costs, slower monetization, or regulation.

I don’t have live market data after June 2024, so treat this as a structural forecast, not a current-price call.

If by “AI bubble bursts” you mean a broad, lasting repricing of AI-linked assets — roughly a 40%+ drop in AI-heavy public/private valuations from peak, with sentiment staying cold for a while — my single best estimate for the next five years is **40%**.

- A lot of the AI boom is concentrated in very profitable incumbents, not just fragile startups.
- There is already real demand: chips, cloud, copilots, and model APIs are generating actual revenue.
- Geopolitics and corporate competition make sustained AI spending more likely.

Why not lower:
- Expectations and capex look ahead of proven monetization.
- Foundation models may be more commoditized than investors expect, which would compress margins.
- Regulation, copyright litigation, safety issues, power/compute constraints, or a recession could trigger a sharp repricing.
- Historically, transformative tech waves often do go through a painful shakeout before long-run value is realized.

So, forced to one number: **40%**.

One extra point: even if Google is first to AGI, that does **not** automatically mean Google stock is the best AI bet. Search cannibalization, capex intensity, regulation, and monetization matter almost as much as technical leadership.

**Best estimate: 45%.**

Assuming “AI bubble burst” means a **broad, sustained repricing** of AI-linked assets driven by disappointment in profits/ROI — not just a normal 15–20% correction — I’d put the odds **within the next five years at about 45%**.

Why that probability is fairly high:
- **Expectations are extremely ambitious.** A lot of valuations implicitly assume very large near-term AI profits and/or AGI-adjacent progress.
- **Capex is enormous.** If the data-center / GPU buildout outruns monetizable demand, markets could reprice fast.
- **Tech hype cycles often overshoot.** Even when the technology is real, markets frequently get the timing wrong.
- **Macro can trigger the pop.** Higher rates, recession, or a credit tightening could hit long-duration AI narratives hard.
- **Regulatory / legal / energy constraints** could slow deployment enough to dent sentiment.

Why it’s not higher than 45%:
- **AI is genuinely useful already.** This is not a purely fictitious story.
- **The main players are profitable incumbents,** not fragile startups dependent on easy capital.
- **Enterprise and government demand is likely to persist** even through setbacks.
- **AI may become infrastructure,** which makes a total dot-com-style collapse less likely.

So my read is: **material risk, but still slightly less likely than not**.

One extra point: for **Google specifically**, the “AI bubble bursts or not” question is only part of the thesis. Google is also a bet on **search durability, cloud execution, and whether AI strengthens or cannibalizes its core business**.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 45%.**

I’m interpreting “the AI bubble bursts” as a broad, lasting repricing of AI-linked stocks and private valuations that breaks the current enthusiasm, not just a routine pullback.

Why the risk is real:
- Current AI valuations and capital spending already assume very strong future profits.
- A lot of today’s revenue is tied to infrastructure buildout, while the end-user economics are still not fully proven.
- Competition, open-source models, and falling model costs could compress margins.
- Over a five-year window, a macro slowdown, higher rates, regulation, copyright liability, or a supply glut are all plausible triggers.
- Historically, even genuinely transformative technologies often go through bubbles and painful resets before the long-run winners emerge.

Why it’s not higher:
- Unlike classic bubbles, many of the main AI beneficiaries are already huge, profitable incumbents with real cash flow.
- AI demand is producing real revenue now in chips, cloud, ads, and software.
- Governments and large enterprises see AI as strategic, which supports spending even if near-term ROI is mixed.

So netting those out, my single best estimate is **45%**. The key swing factor is whether AI delivers clear, durable productivity gains before investor patience with the current spending wave runs out.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 55%**

I’m taking “AI bubble bursts” to mean a broad repricing in the next five years: AI-related public stocks and private frontier/startup valuations falling roughly 40%+ and funding conditions tightening for at least a year.

Why I’d put it **above 50%**:
- **Expectations are very front-loaded.** A lot of current value depends on rapid monetization, sustained capability jumps, and something close to AGI-like progress.
- **Capex is huge.** Spending on chips, data centers, and power is massive relative to still-uncertain long-term margins.
- **Commoditization risk is real.** Open models, fast-following labs, and platform incumbents can squeeze profits even if usage keeps growing.
- **A trigger is easy to imagine.** Slower enterprise adoption, a recession, higher rates, regulation, export controls, or a major safety/backlash event could all cause a reset.

Why I **wouldn’t** put it much higher:
- **The technology is already genuinely useful.** This is not pure vapor; there is real demand and some real productivity gain.
- **The main spenders are unusually strong.** Hyperscalers and governments can keep funding AI longer than typical bubble participants.
- **Transformative tech can stay expensive for a long time.** Even if valuations are stretched, bubbles don’t have to pop quickly.

So my single best estimate is **55%**: slightly more likely than not that there’s a meaningful AI funding/valuation bust within five years, even if AI itself keeps improving.

And for your thesis specifically: that’s only the bubble risk. Your bet also requires **DeepSeek to win technologically and capture value**, which makes the overall investment case materially riskier than 55% implies.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 45%.**

By “burst,” I mean a sharp, lasting repricing of AI-linked valuations and a meaningful pullback in funding appetite, not just a normal correction.

Why I put it that high:
- Expectations already embed very aggressive assumptions about capability progress, monetization, and margins.
- There is a real risk of overbuilding compute and infrastructure before demand and profits fully catch up.
- Frontier models may commoditize faster than investors expect, with open-source and competition compressing returns.
- Over a five-year window, the odds of some macro, regulatory, or geopolitical shock are substantial.

Why I don’t put it higher:
- AI is already producing real value, real adoption, and real revenue.
- Much of the spending is being done by cash-rich incumbents and governments, which makes this boom sturdier than many past tech manias.
- Falling costs should keep expanding use cases, which gives the sector a stronger fundamental floor than purely narrative-driven bubbles had.

So my bottom line is: **a burst is a serious risk, but still a bit less likely than not**. The key lesson from past manias is that a bubble can burst even when the technology itself is genuinely transformative.

One important caveat: for a **DeepSeek-specific** bet, the practical risk is somewhat worse than the sector-wide number, because frontier labs are more exposed to valuation swings, competition, geopolitics, and financing conditions than the broader AI ecosystem.

rows.answer:
Using information available through mid-2024, my **single best-estimate probability is 55%**.

That assumes “the AI bubble bursts” means a **broad, sustained repricing**—roughly: AI-linked public stocks fall ~40%+ from peak and private AI valuations/funding fall ~50%+ for at least a year.

Why I’m above 50%:
- **Valuations and capex already price in very aggressive outcomes.** A lot of money is being spent now on the assumption that monetization will catch up fast.
- **Foundation-model economics may disappoint.** Competition, open source, and commoditization could crush margins even if the tech keeps improving.
- **Five years is a long window.** A recession, higher-for-longer rates, regulation, export controls, or a geopolitical shock could easily puncture sentiment.

Why I’m not much higher:
- **This isn’t pure vapor.** AI already has real use cases and revenue.
- **The key players are unusually strong.** Unlike the dot-com era, many of the biggest AI spenders/winners are cash-rich incumbents, not fragile startups.
- **Even if the hype resets, the technology can keep compounding.**

So my bottom line is: **real technology, but a meaningful chance of market overpricing and a sharp reset. Best estimate: 55%.**

If you’re specifically thinking about DeepSeek, note that **private-market liquidity, dilution, governance, and China/export-control risk** may matter as much as the bubble itself.

rows.answer:
My best estimate is **45%**.

That assumes a fairly strict meaning of “bubble burst”: AI-linked public stocks and private startup valuations falling on the order of 50%+, funding getting much tighter, and the near-term AGI narrative cooling sharply.

Why it’s not lower:
- Current pricing bakes in very large future profits.
- Monetization is still uneven relative to the capex being spent.
- Model-provider moats may prove weaker than expected if open models and cheaper inference commoditize the layer DeepSeek competes in.
- Rates, recession, regulation, or copyright/liability shocks could trigger a repricing.

Why it’s not higher:
- Unlike many bubbles, AI already has real demand and real revenue.
- Much of the spending is being done by cash-rich incumbents, not just speculative startups.
- Governments and big firms see AI as strategic, which can keep capital flowing.
- Even if AGI arrives later than hype suggests, narrower AI may still justify a lot of investment.

So: **45%**.  
For a DeepSeek bet specifically, your practical risk is higher than 45%, because company-specific execution, private-market liquidity, and geopolitical risk all sit on top of the bubble risk.

rows.answer:
Using a practical definition of “the AI bubble bursts” — a broad, sustained repricing where AI-linked public and private valuations fall roughly 50% and funding tightens for at least a year — my single best-estimate probability is **55% within the next five years**.

Why I put it above 50%:
- **Valuations already reflect very aggressive expectations** about AGI timing, monetization, and durable profit margins.
- **AI capex is enormous** relative to proven end-user willingness to pay; that creates overbuild risk.
- **Competition and open-source models** can make model-layer economics worse than investors expect.
- **Hype-cycle history** says transformative technologies often get a real boom and a real bust, even when the tech is genuine.
- **Macro, regulation, or geopolitics** could puncture sentiment quickly.

Why not much higher:
- AI is **not purely speculative**; there is real demand, real revenue, and real productivity value already.
- The main spenders are often **cash-rich hyperscalers and governments**, which makes the sector sturdier than dot-com-era startups.
- Even after a correction, AI development would likely continue.

So: **55%**.  
That’s a forecast of a valuation/funding bust, not a forecast that AI progress stops.

**Best estimate: 45%.**

I’m interpreting “AI bubble burst” as a severe, sustained sector-wide repricing and funding pullback, not just a normal correction. I don’t have live post-mid-2024 market data, so treat this as a structural forecast rather than a read on current prices.

Why it’s **not higher**:
- AI already has real revenue, real adoption, and obvious strategic value.
- The biggest spenders are cash-rich hyperscalers and governments, not just retail/speculative capital.
- Falling model and inference costs should keep creating real use cases.

Why it’s **not lower**:
- A lot of current enthusiasm still depends on very aggressive AGI and monetization assumptions.
- Capex could easily outrun near-term economic returns.
- Frontier models may commoditize faster than investors expect.
- A macro, regulatory, or geopolitical shock could puncture sentiment quickly.

So my single best estimate is **45%**: a substantial risk, but not quite my base case. Also, even if DeepSeek were first to AGI, that still wouldn’t guarantee a good investment outcome—transformative technologies can be real and still go through a bubble bust before the long-run winners are clear.

rows.answer:
Not personalized investment advice, but if by **“AI bubble burst”** you mean a **clear, broad repricing**—say **AI-linked public/private valuations falling ~50% or more, with funding and capex tightening for at least a year**—my **single best estimate is 60% within the next five years**.

Why I put it above 50%:
- **Expectations are extremely high.** A lot of AI pricing assumes very large, fairly near-term profits.
- **Capex is running ahead of proven ROI.** If enterprise monetization lags, hyperscalers and investors could pull back hard.
- **Commoditization risk is real.** Model performance is converging, open-source is strong, and application-layer moats look weak in many areas.
- **History is against “straight-line up.”** Big transformative technologies often create real value *and* still go through a bubble/bust phase.

Why I don’t put it even higher:
- **AI demand is real, not fictional.** Unlike many past manias, there is genuine adoption and revenue.
- **The main spenders are cash-rich giants.** That makes the ecosystem more resilient than a startup-only boom.
- **Costs are falling fast.** Cheaper inference/training can turn shaky economics into workable ones.

So: **60%**.

Also, one important caveat: **“first to AGI” and “best investment” are not the same thing**. Even if DeepSeek won technically, investor returns could still disappoint because of valuation, dilution, geopolitics, regulation, or weak ability to capture the value created.

rows.answer:
If by “AI bubble bursts” you mean a broad, sustained repricing — roughly **50%+ down** in AI-linked public/private valuations plus a real funding slowdown — my **single best-estimate probability over the next five years is 58%**.

Why it’s above 50:
- **Valuations already price in huge success.** A lot of AI names are being valued on very aggressive adoption, margin, and moat assumptions.
- **Capex is outrunning proven monetization.** Enormous spend on chips, data centers, and training only works if revenue and productivity gains show up fast enough.
- **Moats may be weaker than investors hope.** Model commoditization, open-source progress, and platform dependence can crush margins.
- **History rhymes.** Transformative technologies often are real *and* still go through a dot-com-style shakeout.

Why it isn’t much higher:
- **AI is already useful.** This is not pure vapor; there is real demand and real economic value.
- **The biggest spenders are strong balance-sheet players.** Hyperscalers and governments can keep the boom going longer than a typical speculative cycle.
- **A shakeout may be selective, not universal.** Apps and model labs may get hit harder than chips/cloud/infrastructure.

So my base case is: **AI remains important, but markets are slightly more likely than not to have priced too much too soon.**

**Best estimate: 58%.**

And one extra point: even if DeepSeek reached AGI first, that still wouldn’t guarantee it’s the best investment outcome; value could accrue instead to compute suppliers, cloud platforms, or be constrained by geopolitics/regulation.

rows.answer:
Assuming “burst” means the kind of event that would really hurt a DeepSeek-style bet — a broad 50%+ repricing of frontier-AI valuations plus a funding pullback lasting at least a year — my **single best-estimate probability is 55% within the next five years**.

Why it’s that high:
- **Valuations are pricing in very fast, very large profits** that may take longer to materialize.
- **Frontier-model economics are still unproven**: training and inference are expensive, and margins may get squeezed.
- **Open-source and competition can commoditize models**, which is bad for lab-level valuations.
- **A macro shock** (rates, recession, credit tightening) or **policy shock** (regulation, copyright, export controls) could trigger a reset.

Why it’s not higher:
- **AI already has real utility and real revenue**, unlike many pure-speculation bubbles.
- **Much of the spending is from cash-rich hyperscalers and governments**, not just retail mania.
- **A genuine capability jump** could make current expectations look less extreme.

So: **55%**.

One important caveat: **even if DeepSeek were first to AGI, that still wouldn’t guarantee a great investment outcome**. A technology can win while its early equity investors do badly if the value gets captured by cloud platforms, chipmakers, or downstream applications instead of the lab itself.

rows.answer:
If by “AI bubble burst” you mean a broad repricing where AI-related public stocks and private valuations fall roughly 50%+ from a peak and stay depressed for at least a year, my single best-estimate probability over the next five years is **55%**.

Why that’s slightly above 50%:

- **Expectations are very high.** A lot of current pricing assumes sustained rapid progress, strong monetization, and eventual winner-take-most economics.
- **Capex is enormous.** The industry is spending at a pace that could look excessive if revenue and productivity gains arrive more slowly than hoped.
- **AGI timelines are highly uncertain.** If progress hits diminishing returns or stalls short of what markets are pricing in, valuations could reset hard.
- **Competition may crush margins.** Even if AI adoption keeps growing, open-source models and cheaper rivals can make the tech hugely valuable for users but less profitable for investors.
- **Macro/regulatory/geopolitical shock risk is real.** Rates, recession, export controls, energy constraints, and safety regulation could all trigger a repricing.

Why it’s not much higher:

- **This is not pure vapor.** Unlike the dot-com era, the core AI beneficiaries already have real revenue, cash flow, and balance sheets.
- **AI is already useful.** Even if AGI takes longer, there is genuine enterprise demand for models, chips, cloud, and automation.
- **A bubble can deflate gradually.** Earnings growth could partially “grow into” valuations instead of producing a sudden crash.

So my bottom line is: **slightly more likely than not**.

One extra caution: even if DeepSeek or another lab gets to AGI first, **technology success and investment success are not the same thing**. The AI bubble could burst even while AI itself keeps advancing.

rows.answer:
If by “AI bubble burst” you mean a broad 50%+ repricing in AI-linked public valuations plus a sharp, lasting pullback in private AI funding, my single best-estimate probability over the next five years is **60%**.

Why that high:
- **Transformative technologies often still have investment bubbles.** Railroads, telecom, and the internet all mattered enormously and still saw brutal repricings.
- **AI capex is running ahead of proven durable profits.** A lot of spending assumes very large future margins and near-AGI-like outcomes.
- **Value capture is uncertain.** Even if AI keeps improving, model labs may get squeezed by open source, price competition, cloud/platform incumbents, and high compute costs.
- **Five years is a long enough window for a macro shock.** A recession, higher rates, regulation, export controls, or legal setbacks could puncture sentiment fast.

Why not higher:
- **AI already has real utility and real revenue.** This is not purely narrative-driven hype.
- **The biggest spenders are cash-rich incumbents and governments, not just fragile speculators.** That makes a full collapse less likely.
- **If capability gains stay rapid, fundamentals may catch up to valuations instead of breaking beneath them.**

So: **60%**.

One extra point: even if DeepSeek were first to AGI, that would not guarantee a great investment outcome. A bubble can burst before the winner monetizes, and equity holders can still lose through dilution, geopolitics, regulation, or weak value capture.

rows.answer:
Not personal financial advice, but my single best estimate is **55%**.

If by “AI bubble burst” you mean a major sector-wide valuation reset and funding pullback, I think it’s **slightly more likely than not** within the next five years.

Main reasons it’s that high:
- Current valuations in parts of AI assume very fast capability gains and very large future profits.
- A lot of frontier AI still has unclear long-term unit economics and moats.
- Open-source models and platform competition can compress margins for model labs.
- Over a five-year window, recession, higher rates, regulation, safety backlash, or geopolitics could easily trigger a sharp repricing.

Main reasons it’s not higher:
- AI is already genuinely useful; this is not pure vapor.
- Big customers and governments are spending real money.
- Much of the buildout is funded by very strong balance sheets, which can keep the cycle going longer than a typical bubble.

So my base case is: **55% chance of a real AI bubble burst within five years**. Also, for a DeepSeek-style bet, total risk is higher than that, because sector bubble risk is only one layer; being first to AGI and being the best investment are not the same thing.

rows.answer:
If by “AI bubble burst” you mean a broad, lasting repricing of AI-linked assets and funding conditions—not just a normal correction—my single best estimate is **55%** over the next five years.

Why I put it slightly above even odds:
- **Capex is running ahead of proven ROI.** A lot of spending still rests on future expectations rather than current profits.
- **Frontier-model economics are shaky.** Training/inference costs are huge, competition is intense, and open/cheap models can compress margins.
- **History is unkind to hot general-purpose technologies.** The tech can be real and still have a painful bubble unwind.
- **Macro/regulatory/geopolitical risk matters.** Recession, higher rates, AI regulation, or export-control escalation could all trigger a repricing.

Why it’s not higher:
- **AI demand is real already.** This is not pure vaporware.
- **The biggest AI spenders are cash-rich incumbents.** That makes the setup sturdier than 1999-style dot-coms.
- **Governments treat AI as strategic infrastructure.** That supports spending even if commercial returns lag.

So: **55%**.

One extra caution: even if DeepSeek were first to AGI, that still wouldn’t guarantee investor returns—value capture, dilution, competition, and geopolitics matter almost as much as the technology outcome.

rows.answer:
Based on the market setup I know through mid-2024, my single best estimate is **45%**.

That assumes “AI bubble burst” means a broad, sustained repricing of AI-linked public/private valuations and a sharp pullback in funding, not just a normal correction.

Why that probability is substantial:
- Valuations and capex are running ahead of proven end-customer ROI.
- A lot of the upside case implicitly assumes AGI-adjacent outcomes or very fast enterprise adoption.
- Core-model economics could commoditize faster than expected because of competition, open source, and falling inference costs.
- The sector is exposed to rates, recession, regulation, export controls, and power/compute constraints.

Why it’s not higher:
- AI already has real usage, real revenue, and real productivity gains.
- The biggest spenders are cash-rich hyperscalers and governments, which makes the cycle more durable than a typical speculative mania.
- Fundamentals may partially “grow into” today’s prices even if AGI arrives later than bulls expect.

One important caveat: AI progress and AI investment returns are different things. A private frontier lab like DeepSeek could be technically successful and still be a poor investment because of dilution, competition, liquidity, or geopolitics.

rows.answer:
If by “the AI bubble bursts” you mean a broad, sustained repricing of AI-related public and private valuations—roughly a 50%+ drawdown in the hot part of the sector, plus a real funding pullback—my best estimate for the next five years is **45%**.

Why not higher: AI demand is real, costs keep falling, the biggest spenders are cash-rich hyperscalers rather than purely speculative startups, and governments have strategic reasons to keep the sector funded.

Why not lower: current expectations already price in very strong monetization and continued capability gains; capex could outrun demand; frontier models are commoditizing quickly; and over a five-year horizon the odds of some recession, rates shock, regulatory shock, or geopolitical disruption are significant. Also, bubbles often burst even when the underlying technology is genuinely transformative.

A separate caution: even if DeepSeek reached AGI first, that would not guarantee strong investor returns—value can be competed away, diluted, regulated, or captured elsewhere in the stack.

**Single best-estimate probability: 45%.**

rows.answer:
Best estimate: **60%**.

I’m using “AI bubble bursts” to mean a **sharp, broad repricing of AI-related valuations and a real pullback in funding/spending**, not “AI turns out to be fake.”

Why I put it above even odds:
- **Expectations are extremely high.** Current AI valuations embed very fast capability gains and very fast monetization.
- **Capex is enormous, ROI is still uneven.** If buyers don’t show clear returns quickly enough, markets can re-rate hard.
- **Competition may destroy margins.** Even if AI usage explodes, model providers may not capture much of the value because of open source, price wars, and commoditization.
- **Five years is a long time.** A recession, tighter capital, regulation, geopolitics, or a slowdown in model progress could easily trigger a bust.
- **History rhymes.** Transformative technologies often are real and still go through a painful hype unwind before fundamentals catch up.

Why I don’t put it higher:
- **AI already has real utility.** This is not a pure fantasy sector.
- **The main spenders are unusually strong.** Big tech can keep investing through downturns, which lowers the odds of a total collapse.
- **Governments and enterprises see AI as strategic.** That gives the sector a demand floor.

So my base case is: **AI probably keeps advancing, but there’s a better-than-even chance the market enthusiasm resets sharply within five years.** If your DeepSeek thesis only works if that reset does *not* happen, it’s a fairly fragile thesis.

rows.answer:
My single best estimate is **45%**.

I’m taking “AI bubble bursts” to mean **more than a normal correction**: a broad, sustained repricing of AI-linked assets, a sharp pullback in private funding, and a meaningful downgrade to expected AI profits.

Why it’s that high:
- **Valuations and capex are running ahead of proven monetization.**
- **Many AI products may end up with weak moats**, especially if models commoditize and pricing power collapses.
- **Five years is a long window** for a recession, higher-rate shock, regulation, export controls, or geopolitics to puncture the narrative.
- **Historically, transformative technologies often do have boom-bust cycles**, even when the technology itself is real.

Why it’s not higher:
- **AI already has real utility** in coding, search, support, content workflows, and enterprise automation.
- **Costs are falling fast**, which can turn today’s hype into tomorrow’s actual adoption.
- A lot of the spending is coming from **cash-rich incumbents**, not just fragile speculative startups, which makes this healthier than a pure dot-com-style setup.

So: **slightly less than a coin flip** for a true sector-wide bust within five years.

One extra point: even if DeepSeek reached AGI first, that **wouldn’t automatically mean it captures the investment value**; single-company, private-market, and geopolitical risks could matter as much as the bubble risk.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 55%.**

That assumes “burst” means a real, sustained repricing of AI-related valuations and funding, not just a normal tech correction.

Why I put it slightly **above even**:
- Parts of the AI market—especially frontier labs and some startups—are priced on very large AGI-era profit assumptions that are far from proven.
- The sector is extremely capital-intensive, and the business case for all that spend is still not fully validated.
- Margins could get squeezed by open-source models, competition, and falling model prices.
- A macro slowdown, higher rates, regulation, copyright rulings, export controls, or geopolitics could be the trigger.

Why I **don’t** put it much higher:
- Unlike classic bubbles, a lot of the core AI spending is coming from highly profitable incumbents.
- There is already real demand, real usage, and real revenue—not just a story.
- Governments and hyperscalers treat AI as strategic, which makes the funding cycle more durable than a typical speculative mania.

The key point is that **“AI becomes transformative” and “AI valuations never crack” are not the same bet**. World-changing technologies often still go through bubbles.

So if you want one number: **55% chance the AI bubble bursts within five years**.

And for a DeepSeek-specific bet, your effective risk is higher than that, because bubble risk is only one of several ways the investment can go wrong.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 45%.**

Assuming “the AI bubble bursts” means a **sector-wide, sustained valuation reset** — roughly a **40%+ drop in leading AI-linked assets plus a sharp pullback in funding/capex**, not just ordinary volatility — I’d put the odds **within the next five years at about 45%**.

Why that high:
- **Valuations still embed very aggressive assumptions** about AGI timing, monetization, and long-term margins.
- **A lot of AI value may get competed away.** Open-source models, cheaper inference, and rapid imitation can make AI hugely useful while making it hard for any one company to earn extraordinary profits.
- **The buildout is capex-heavy.** If enterprise ROI comes in slower than expected, spending can reverse fast.
- **Macro, regulation, copyright, or geopolitics** could easily trigger a broad de-rating.

Why not higher:
- **This isn’t pure fantasy.** AI already has real users, real revenue, and real productivity gains.
- **The main spenders are cash-rich hyperscalers and governments**, not just weak companies funded by retail hype.
- **AI is a real general-purpose technology**, so there’s a decent chance fundamentals grow into current expectations instead of snapping.

So my view is: **a true AI-bubble bust is a little less likely than not, but close**.

One extra point: even if the bubble **doesn’t** burst, a bet on **DeepSeek specifically** still carries huge **company-specific, competitive, and geopolitical risk**. “First to AGI” and “best investment outcome” are not the same thing.

rows.answer:
If by “AI bubble burst” you mean a broad, sustained AI-specific valuation reset — not just a normal correction, but something like major multiple compression, funding drying up, and hyperscaler/VC pullbacks because expectations outran reality — my best estimate is **40%** within the next five years.

Why it’s that high:
- Current AI valuations and capex imply very strong monetization and continued rapid capability gains.
- Enterprise adoption may be real but slower and messier than the hype assumes.
- Model-layer economics look fragile: open-source and cheaper competitors can commoditize the product even if usage explodes.
- A 5-year window is long enough for a recession, higher-real-rate episode, or regulatory/geopolitical shock to puncture sentiment.

Why it’s not higher:
- AI already has genuine utility; this is not purely vapor.
- Falling inference costs should expand usage even if margins compress.
- The biggest AI spenders are cash-rich incumbents, not mostly unprofitable startups.
- Governments increasingly view AI as strategic, which supports continued investment.

So my single-number forecast is **40%**.

One important caveat: a bet on **DeepSeek specifically** is riskier than this sector-level number suggests. Even if the AI bubble does **not** burst, DeepSeek still has to win technically, be investable on good terms, navigate geopolitics, and actually capture economic value rather than helping commoditize the model layer.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 45%.**

Assuming “AI bubble bursts” means a sharp, sustained repricing of AI-related valuations and a real pullback in funding, I’d put the odds over the next five years at about **45%**.

Why that risk is meaningful:
- AI capex and startup valuations are running ahead of clearly proven end-user ROI.
- Current pricing assumes very fast adoption, durable moats, and continued frontier-model progress.
- Open-source and lower-cost models can increase usage while compressing margins, which is bad for many current valuation stories.
- A recession, higher-for-longer rates, regulation, export controls, IP litigation, or energy/compute bottlenecks could trigger a fast sentiment reversal.

Why it isn’t higher:
- AI already has real utility, real revenue, and clear enterprise demand.
- The biggest spenders are cash-rich incumbents and governments, making this less fragile than a classic startup-only bubble.
- Some of the excess can deflate gradually as earnings catch up, instead of collapsing all at once.

So: **45%**.  
The main caution for your DeepSeek thesis is that even if AI changes the world, and even if DeepSeek is technically excellent, investors can still lose if value capture shifts elsewhere or geopolitics intervenes.

rows.answer:
If by “burst” you mean a major, lasting repricing of AI-related assets—not just a normal 20% correction—my single best estimate is **55%** over the next five years.

Why it’s that high:
- Current valuations in parts of AI imply very aggressive assumptions about monetization, margins, and AGI-speed progress.
- A lot of spending is on infrastructure and model development ahead of clearly proven end-demand, which creates classic overbuild risk.
- Competition, especially open-source and fast model commoditization, may erode moats and pricing power.
- Historically, transformative technologies often do have bubbles that burst even when the technology itself is real.

Why it’s not higher:
- Unlike dot-com 1999, many of the biggest AI players are already huge, profitable companies with real cash flow.
- AI already has genuine use cases and revenue, so this is not pure story stock behavior.
- Falling costs and improving capabilities could let real demand catch up to current investment.
- Geopolitical and strategic pressures make a total funding freeze less likely.

So my base case is: **slightly more likely than not** that there’s a meaningful AI bubble burst within five years.

One extra note: for a DeepSeek-style bet, the practical risk is probably **higher** than this broad-market number, because you’re adding company-specific, governance, dilution, and geopolitical risk on top of the sector bubble risk.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 45%.**

Assuming by **“AI bubble bursts”** you mean something like a **sector-wide 50%+ valuation reset** in AI-linked public/private assets, plus a real pullback in funding and sentiment, I’d put the odds **within the next five years at about 45%**.

Why it’s that high:
- **Valuations already price in very large future wins**, in some cases implicitly assuming near-AGI economics.
- **Capex is enormous**; if demand or monetization lags, overbuild can trigger a sharp repricing.
- **Enterprise ROI is still uneven**; lots of experimentation, less proof of durable margins.
- **Competition and open-source** could commoditize parts of the stack and crush profits.
- A **recession / higher rates / regulation / IP issues / power constraints** could puncture sentiment fast.

Why it’s not higher:
- Unlike many classic bubbles, a lot of AI spending is being led by **cash-rich incumbents** with real businesses.
- **AI already has genuine utility and revenue**, even without AGI.
- This may be a real **general-purpose technology wave**, which can survive overvaluation better than pure hype.

So my base case is: **a true bust is plausible but not the most likely outcome**.  
If you mean a **smaller correction** rather than a full “burst,” the probability is **much higher**.

Also: for **OpenAI specifically**, “first to AGI” does **not** automatically translate into the best investor return; structure, dilution, governance, and monetization matter too.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 45%.**

Assuming “the AI bubble bursts” means a dot-com-style, broad and sustained reset in AI valuations and funding conditions—not just normal volatility—I’d put the chance over the next five years at **45%**.

Why it’s that high:
- Current prices and spending imply very optimistic timelines for AGI, productivity gains, and profit capture.
- The economics of frontier model companies are still unsettled: huge capex, unclear long-run margins, and real commoditization risk.
- A recession, rates shock, or major regulation/safety event could puncture sentiment fast.

Why it’s not higher:
- AI already has real users, real revenue, and real enterprise value.
- The biggest AI spenders are cash-rich incumbents, not just fragile startups.
- Governments and large firms see AI as strategic, which gives the sector a stronger floor than past hype cycles.

So: **a burst is a serious risk, but not my base case**. If your OpenAI bet only works if the bubble stays intact, you’re relying on something that’s only slightly better than a coin flip. The tech can win even if the bubble bursts.

rows.answer:
I don’t have live market data past June 2024, so treat this as a structural forecast, not a current tape read.

If by “the AI bubble bursts” you mean a sustained ~50%+ markdown in late-stage private AI valuations and a broad derating of AI-heavy public names, my single best-estimate probability over the next five years is **55%**.

Why it’s above 50%:
- History says real transformative technologies often still go through financing bubbles and sharp repricings.
- Current AI valuations embed very optimistic assumptions about AGI timing, market size, and long-run margins.
- Capex is being pulled forward faster than monetization is being proven.
- Even if AI works, competition and commoditization could crush profits and valuations.
- Five years is long enough for a recession, rate shock, or regulatory/safety/IP shock to trigger a reset.

Why it’s not higher:
- AI already has real utility and real revenue; this isn’t pure vapor.
- The biggest spenders are cash-rich incumbents, which makes the boom more durable than a classic startup-only mania.
- Strategic/geopolitical incentives could keep frontier AI funded even through disappointment.

So my best estimate is **55%**: slightly more likely than not that AI market valuations crack materially within five years, even if AI itself keeps improving.

And for an OpenAI bet specifically, bubble risk is only one part of it; governance, dilution, liquidity, and whether outside investors actually capture AGI upside matter too.

I don’t have live market data past mid-2024, so treat this as a structural forecast, not a real-time market call.

Interpreting “the AI bubble bursts” as a genuine reset — roughly a 50%+ repricing in AI-heavy valuations and/or severe private-market markdowns because monetization or AGI timelines disappoint — my single best estimate for the next five years is **45%**.

Why it’s that high:
- Current enthusiasm prices in very strong assumptions about near-term AI progress, durable moats, and returns on huge capex.
- Frontier-model economics are still not fully proven and could get squeezed by competition, open-source, and falling differentiation.
- History says transformative technologies often still experience bubbles that pop before fundamentals catch up.
- A macro shock, higher-for-longer rates, regulation, or a visible slowdown in capability gains could trigger a reset.

Why it’s not higher:
- AI already has real users, real revenue, and real enterprise value.
- The biggest spenders are cash-rich incumbents and governments, not just fragile startups.
- AI does not need full AGI to justify substantial spending; steady incremental adoption could let fundamentals catch up.

So: **45%**.

The key distinction is that “AI will matter enormously” and “current AI valuations are sustainable” are different claims. A bubble can burst even if OpenAI is on the right side of the technology.

rows.answer:
My single best estimate: **45%**.

I’m using **“bubble burst”** to mean a **broad, sustained re-pricing of AI valuations and funding conditions**—enough to materially hurt frontier-lab valuations and make capital much harder to raise—not just a normal tech pullback.

Why that probability is fairly high:
- **Valuations already price in a lot**: fast capability gains, strong monetization, and durable moats.
- **Capex is enormous relative to proven end-demand**: if revenue lags the buildout of chips, data centers, and model spending, sentiment can reverse hard.
- **Economics are still unsettled**: inference costs, competition, open-source pressure, and unclear application-layer margins could compress profits.
- **History cuts against complacency**: railroads, telecom, and dot-coms all involved real transformative technologies that still went through painful bubbles.
- **Macro/regulatory triggers are plausible**: recession, higher rates, copyright rulings, safety regulation, export controls, or energy bottlenecks.

Why it’s not higher:
- **AI is already delivering real value**, unlike many classic bubbles at the same stage.
- **The biggest spenders are cash-rich hyperscalers**, not just speculative retail/VC money, which makes the boom more durable.
- **Geopolitical and strategic incentives** mean governments and large firms may keep spending even through disappointing quarters.
- **Even if many AI companies disappoint, the technology itself is probably not a fad**.

So I’d treat an AI-bubble burst in the next five years as a **serious risk, but not quite the base case**.

One extra caution: **betting on OpenAI is not the same as betting on AGI**. Even if OpenAI reaches AGI first and the bubble doesn’t burst, your return still depends on **investment terms, dilution, governance, and who actually captures the economics**.

rows.answer:
**My best estimate: 40%.**

That assumes “AI bubble bursts” means a broad, sentiment-driven reset in AI valuations and funding, not just a normal correction.

Why I put it that high:
- Current AI valuations often bake in very aggressive assumptions about capability progress, monetization, and winner-take-most profits.
- The industry is spending enormous sums on chips, data centers, and talent before many use cases have clearly proven durable ROI.
- AI may become hugely useful while still becoming less profitable than investors expect, because of competition, open-source models, and commoditization.
- A recession, higher-for-longer rates, regulation, or IP/liability shocks could hit a hype-heavy sector hard and fast.

Why I don’t put it higher:
- Unlike many classic bubbles, AI already has real users, real revenue, and real productivity gains.
- The key backers are mostly cash-rich hyperscalers and governments, not just fragile startups dependent on easy money.
- Costs are falling and products are improving, so the sector has a plausible path to “grow into” lofty expectations instead of collapsing.
- Even if frontier-model companies re-rate sharply, AI infrastructure and applied software could keep the broader theme from fully imploding.

So my bottom line is: **substantial risk, but not the base case — 40%.**

One extra note: if your actual bet is specifically **OpenAI**, your risk of disappointment may be higher than that, because bubble risk is only one part of the story; governance, dilution, competition, and whether AGI value actually accrues to outside investors also matter.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 55%.**

I’m interpreting “the AI bubble bursts” as a **clear, sector-wide valuation reset and funding pullback**, not just normal volatility.

Why I put it **slightly above even odds**:

- **Valuations and spending are pricing in a lot of optimism**: very fast model progress, fast enterprise adoption, and strong long-term profits.
- **Capex is running ahead of fully proven ROI** in many use cases. If customers don’t get enough value fast enough, sentiment can turn quickly.
- **Competition may compress margins**: open models, cloud providers, and model commoditization could mean the ultimate profit pool is smaller than today’s valuations imply.
- **A macro slowdown, higher rates, regulation, copyright/legal setbacks, or a safety backlash** could be the trigger.

Why it’s **not higher**:

- **AI is not pure vapor**. It already has real demand, real revenue, and real productivity value.
- **The biggest AI spenders are giant, cash-rich firms and governments**, which makes the boom more durable than many past speculative manias.
- **Costs are falling and use cases are broad**, so fundamentals may catch up to some of the hype.

So my single best estimate is **55% that the AI bubble bursts within five years**.

One important caveat: **AI can be transformative and still have a bubble burst**. Dot-com is the classic example. So an OpenAI investment is not just a bet on “they reach AGI first”; it’s also a bet on **valuation, timing, competitive economics, and the exact terms of the investment**.

rows.answer:
Best estimate: **45%**

I’m interpreting “the AI bubble bursts” as a **broad, sharp repricing** over the next five years: AI-linked valuations fall hard, private funding tightens, and major AI capex plans get cut.

Why I’m **not higher** than 45%:
- AI already has **real revenue, real users, and real productivity use cases**.
- The biggest spenders are mostly **cash-rich hyperscalers**, not just fragile startups.
- There’s a **strategic/geopolitical floor** under AI spending from governments and large platforms.

Why I’m **not lower**:
- Current valuations and infrastructure spending still assume **very large future profits** that are not yet fully proven.
- AI model economics remain uncertain: **high compute costs, heavy competition, and margin compression** are real risks.
- A lot of enthusiasm is tied to **AGI expectations**; if progress slows or disappoints, sentiment could turn fast.
- **Macro shocks, regulation, copyright/IP setbacks, or power/supply constraints** could trigger a reversal.
- Historically, even **genuinely transformative technologies** often go through a bubble and bust phase before long-term success.

So, if forced to give one number: **45% chance of a meaningful AI bubble burst within five years**.

One important caveat: even if OpenAI is first to AGI, that **doesn’t automatically mean the investment pays off**. Entry valuation, deal terms, liquidity, governance, and who captures the economics matter just as much.

**Best estimate: 45%.**

I’m interpreting “the AI bubble bursts” as a broad, sustained reset in AI valuations and funding — e.g. major drawdowns across AI-linked public/private assets, much tighter financing for frontier labs, and a narrative shift from “AGI soon, huge profits” to “overbuilt/overhyped.”

Why the risk is that high:
- A lot of current value rests on very large future profits that are not yet proven.
- Frontier AI is extremely capital-intensive; if progress slows or enterprise ROI disappoints, funding can tighten quickly.
- Capex may be outrunning realized demand, which is classic bubble behavior.
- A recession, higher-for-longer rates, regulation, or a major safety/liability event could be the trigger.

Why I don’t put it above 50%:
- Unlike many bubbles, AI already has real users, real revenue, and real utility.
- The biggest spenders are deep-pocketed incumbents and governments, which makes the ecosystem sturdier than a pure startup mania.
- Costs keep falling, so adoption can widen even if AGI arrives later than bulls expect.

So my single-number forecast is **45%**: not the base case, but far too high to treat as a tail risk.

Also: an OpenAI investment is not a pure “first to AGI” bet — deal terms, dilution, governance, and partner economics matter almost as much as the technology outcome.

Best estimate: **45%**.

I’m treating “AI bubble burst” as a **broad, sustained repricing** — roughly **50%+ declines** in major AI-linked public/private valuations plus a clear funding/sentiment reset — not just ordinary tech volatility.

Why I’m **below 50%**:
- AI already has **real demand**, real revenue, and some genuine productivity gains.
- The biggest spenders are mostly **cash-rich incumbents** and governments, not just fragile startups.
- Costs are still falling fast, which can turn marginal use cases into economic ones over time.

Why I’m still **as high as 45%**:
- Current valuations often assume **very large future profits** and durable moats.
- A lot of AI spending still looks like an **arms race**; monetization may lag capex.
- Competition and open-source could **commoditize** much of the stack, squeezing margins.
- A **macro/rates shock**, regulatory/copyright shock, safety incident, or overbuild in compute could puncture sentiment.
- Historically, **transformative technologies still often have bubbles that pop** before the long-run winners fully emerge.

So my single-number view is: **45% chance of an AI-bubble burst within five years**.

One extra caution: if you mean **OpenAI specifically**, your investment risk is probably **higher than that headline number**, because even “first to AGI” doesn’t guarantee investor upside if governance, deal terms, dilution, or regulation limit value capture.

rows.answer:
Assuming by “AI bubble burst” you mean a dot-com-style repricing — AI-related public/private valuations falling roughly 50%+ from peak, with fundraising and capex pulling back hard for at least a year — my **single best-estimate probability is 40% within the next five years**.

Why it’s that high:
- Current AI valuations embed very aggressive assumptions about both capability progress and monetization.
- The sector is extremely capital-intensive; if revenue lags infrastructure spend, investors can re-rate it fast.
- Model-layer economics may be weaker than the market hopes because of competition, open-source pressure, and uncertain moats.
- Regulatory, copyright, safety, geopolitical, or macro shocks could easily trigger a reset.
- Historically, real transformative technologies still often go through bubbles and busts.

Why it’s not higher:
- AI already has real customers, real revenue, and real productivity gains.
- The biggest spenders are cash-rich hyperscalers and governments, not just speculative retail money.
- AI looks like a genuine general-purpose technology, not pure hype.
- Even if expectations cool, underlying adoption can keep growing, which limits how complete the unwind is likely to be.

So: **meaningful bust risk, but still below even odds**.

One extra caution: even if OpenAI is first to AGI, your return depends not just on AI avoiding a bubble bust, but on OpenAI’s governance, dilution, and whatever economic rights your investment actually gives you.

I don’t have live market data after June 2024, so treat this as a structural forecast, not a current-market call.

If by “AI bubble burst” you mean a broad, sustained repricing—roughly a 50%+ drop in AI-linked public/private valuations plus a meaningful funding pullback—my single best-estimate probability over the next five years is **45%**.

Why that high:
- current valuations embed very aggressive adoption and AGI-timeline assumptions
- AI capex is enormous relative to proven end-user monetization
- competition and open-source could compress margins faster than expected
- macro, rates, regulation, or a safety/legal shock could trigger a de-rating
- real technologies often still produce investor bubbles

Why not higher:
- unlike many classic bubbles, AI already has real utility and real revenue
- the biggest AI spenders are cash-rich incumbents, not fragile speculators
- governments and large firms increasingly see AI as strategic infrastructure

So my best estimate is **45%**. Even if AI is transformative, that does not prevent a bubble from bursting along the way.

rows.answer:
I only have market information through June 2024, so treat this as a structural estimate, not a live market call.

**Best estimate: 45%**

That is, I’d put roughly a **45% chance that the AI bubble materially bursts within the next five years**, where “burst” means a broad, sustained repricing of AI-linked public/private valuations rather than a normal correction.

Why it’s that high:
- **Valuations are pricing in a lot**: fast capability gains, strong monetization, and durable margins.
- **Economics are still shaky** for many AI products: expensive compute, uncertain enterprise ROI, and unclear willingness to pay.
- **Value capture may be weaker than hype assumes**: open-source models, commoditization, and cloud/platform intermediaries could squeeze labs like OpenAI.
- **Shock risk is real**: recession, higher-for-longer rates, regulation, copyright rulings, or power/chip bottlenecks could trigger a reset.

Why it’s not higher:
- **This isn’t pure vapor**: AI already has real users, real revenue, and clear utility.
- **The main spenders are very strong balance sheets**: hyperscalers and governments can keep funding the buildout longer than typical bubble players.
- **Strategic arms-race dynamics** can sustain spending even when near-term ROI is underwhelming.

So my single-number answer is:

**45%**

One extra caution: for an **OpenAI investment specifically**, the chance of disappointing returns is probably **higher than 45%**, because bubble risk is only one layer; valuation, dilution, governance, partner economics, and who actually captures AGI profits matter too.

rows.answer:
Assuming **“AI bubble burst”** means a **broad, sustained repricing** of AI-related assets — not just a normal correction, but something like **major multiple compression, widespread private down rounds, and funding drying up** — my **single best-estimate probability is 45% within the next five years**.

Why that high:

- **Valuations are pricing in very large future outcomes.** A lot of AI value today reflects expectations about AGI, dominant platforms, or huge productivity gains that may take longer to materialize.
- **Capex is enormous relative to proven ROI.** Frontier labs and hyperscalers are spending heavily on chips, data centers, and power. If monetization lags, sentiment can flip fast.
- **Moats are less certain than bulls assume.** Open-source models, model commoditization, and distribution power from big platforms could squeeze economics.
- **Five years is long enough for a macro shock.** A recession, higher-for-longer rates, or tighter credit would hit speculative, long-duration assets hard.
- **There are nontrivial execution risks.** Scaling may slow, regulation may bite, copyright/liability issues may grow, and power/chip bottlenecks could constrain deployment.

Why it’s not higher:

- **AI is real, useful, and already monetizing in some areas.** This is not pure vapor; there are genuine products and customers.
- **The key spenders are unusually strong.** Big tech firms and governments can keep funding AI through periods that would kill weaker bubbles.
- **Private markets can delay the reckoning.** Even if public sentiment cools, private marks often adjust slowly.
- **Earnings may catch up.** If inference gets cheaper and enterprise adoption deepens, today’s valuations may look less extreme over time.

So: **45%**.

If your xAI thesis only works if AI capital markets stay euphoric, that’s a fairly fragile setup — because on this estimate, the odds of a real bust are **not far from a coin flip**.

rows.answer:
My best estimate: **45%**

That assumes **“burst”** means a true sector-wide repricing — roughly **50%+ valuation compression across AI-heavy names/private rounds, plus a meaningful pullback in funding and capex for at least a year** — not just a normal correction.

Why it’s that high:
- **Valuations already price in a lot of future success.** Expectations around AGI, monetization, and productivity gains are extremely aggressive.
- **Capex is enormous.** If the ROI on all the chips, data centers, and model training disappoints, sentiment can turn fast.
- **A lot of the app layer looks commoditized.** If margins collapse and moats prove weak, investors may reassess the whole space.
- **Macro/regulatory shocks matter.** Recession, higher rates, copyright rulings, safety regulation, or geopolitics could trigger a reset.

Why it’s not higher:
- **There is real demand and real utility already.** This isn’t purely vapor; AI is already being used in coding, search, support, enterprise software, and infrastructure.
- **The biggest spenders are cash-rich hyperscalers and governments.** That makes a full collapse less likely than in a startup-only mania.
- **Even if many companies are overvalued, the technology itself is probably durable.** That can support the sector through disappointment.

So my bottom line is: **slightly below even odds of a true AI-bubble burst within five years**.

One extra point: **xAI is probably riskier than “AI” in general**. Even if the overall bubble doesn’t burst, frontier-lab valuations can still get hit hard by dilution, capex needs, competition, or weaker-than-expected moat.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 45%**

That assumes **“the AI bubble bursts”** means a **broad 40%+ repricing of AI-linked public/private valuations plus a real funding pullback**, not just a normal correction.

**Why it could burst:**
- Current valuations embed very aggressive assumptions about monetization, margins, and AGI timelines.
- AI capex is huge; if enterprise ROI lags, sentiment can reverse fast.
- Model providers may get commoditized by competition and open source.
- Big transformative technologies often do have overbuild/crash phases even when the technology itself eventually wins.
- A macro shock, higher-for-longer rates, regulation, or a major safety/legal event could be the trigger.

**Why it might not:**
- Unlike classic bubbles, a lot of AI spending is backed by **very profitable incumbents**.
- AI already has **real demand and real revenue**, especially in chips, cloud, and enterprise tools.
- Falling costs keep opening up new use cases.
- Governments and large firms are likely to keep funding strategically important AI infrastructure even through disappointment cycles.

So my net view is: **meaningful risk, but not more likely than not**.  
For **xAI specifically**, the odds of a painful valuation reset are probably **somewhat higher than 45%**, because late-stage private-company pricing is usually more fragile than the overall sector.

- AI capex is enormous relative to clearly proven end-user ROI.

So: **45%**.  
For a company like xAI, I’d treat the repricing risk as at least that high, and probably somewhat higher, because private AGI-lab valuations are among the most expectation-heavy.

rows.answer:
I don’t have live 2026 market data, so this is a structural forecast, not a call on today’s exact pricing.

**Best estimate: 45%**

That’s assuming **“AI bubble burst”** means a **dot-com-style sector-wide reset**: roughly **50%+ declines in many AI-linked valuations**, sharp private-market markdowns, and a meaningful tightening in new funding.

**Why the probability is that high**
- **Expectations are extreme.** A lot of AI value depends on very optimistic assumptions about AGI timelines, monetization, and durable margins.
- **Capex is enormous.** If revenue and productivity gains lag data-center/GPU spending, the sector can look overbuilt very quickly.
- **Returns may be competed away.** Open-source models, cloud bundling, and fast imitation could make frontier models less profitable than investors expect.
- **Five years is a long window.** Plenty of time for a recession, rate shock, regulation, safety incident, or a technical plateau.

**Why it’s not higher**
- **This is not pure vapor.** AI already has real adoption, real revenue, and clear usefulness.
- **Deep-pocketed backers matter.** Hyperscalers and governments can keep funding AI through periods that would kill a normal bubble.
- **Transformative tech can survive a shakeout.** Even if valuations crack, the underlying industry may keep growing.

So my single best estimate is **45%**: **high enough that a serious shakeout is a real risk, but below 50% because AI has more real demand and strategic support than a classic speculative mania.**

One extra point: for **xAI specifically**, bubble risk is only part of the story. Even if AGI arrives and the bubble doesn’t burst, investor returns still depend on **dilution, governance, competition, and whether value accrues to model labs at all**.

rows.answer:
If by **“AI bubble bursts”** you mean a **broad valuation reset**—roughly **40%+ declines in AI-linked public names, much tighter private funding, and a multi-quarter pullback in AI capex**—my **single best estimate is 60% over the next five years**.

Why I put it above 50%:
- **Valuations are ahead of durable cash flows.** A lot of AI pricing assumes very large future profits that are still uncertain.
- **Capex is huge and front-loaded.** Frontier AI needs massive ongoing spending on chips, data centers, and power; that makes the sector vulnerable if revenue lags.
- **Competition will pressure margins.** Even if AI adoption keeps growing, model and app economics may commoditize faster than investors expect.
- **Five years is a long enough window for a macro shock.** A recession, higher rates, or credit tightening often punctures narrative-driven sectors.

Why it’s not much higher:
- **AI is real, not vapor.** There is genuine demand, real usage, and real revenue in parts of the stack.
- **Strategic buyers can keep the cycle going.** Big tech and governments may continue spending for competitive reasons even if near-term returns are weak.
- **Costs are falling fast.** That can turn hype into real adoption instead of collapse.

So my bottom line is: **60%**.  
That would be a **valuation/funding bust**, not a claim that AI itself fails.

rows.answer:
Assuming “the AI bubble bursts” means a broad, sustained repricing of AI-linked public and private valuations, not just a normal correction, my single best-estimate probability over the next five years is **60%**.

Why I land there:
- Capex and valuation expectations are running ahead of clearly proven monetization.
- The frontier-model layer may turn out to be more commoditized, and less durably profitable, than current pricing assumes.
- Transformative technologies often generate real economic change and a painful investor bust at the same time.
- Macro, rates, regulation, copyright, energy, or safety shocks could all trigger a reset.

Why I’m not higher:
- AI already has real utility, real revenue, and visible productivity value.
- Cash-rich hyperscalers and governments can support the buildout longer than a typical speculative cycle.
- Continued capability gains could let fundamentals catch up before sentiment cracks.

Bottom line: I think a burst is more likely than not, even if AI itself keeps advancing. And for a private frontier lab like xAI, the financing, dilution, and liquidity risks are probably harsher than the broad sector average. Tech success and shareholder returns are not the same thing.

rows.answer:
My best estimate is **45%**.

That assumes “the AI bubble bursts” means a **real, sustained repricing**—not just normal volatility, but something like **40–60%+ declines in the hottest AI names/private valuations, plus a sharp tightening in funding and sentiment**.

Why it’s that high:
- **Valuations in parts of AI are still being set on very optimistic assumptions** about AGI timing, durable moats, and future margins.
- **The capex buildout is enormous**; if the ROI on all that compute/infrastructure comes in below expectations, markets could reprice hard.
- **Commoditization risk is real**: model performance can converge, open-source can pressure pricing, and application-layer margins may disappoint.
- A **macro shock, rate shock, recession, regulation, or safety incident** could puncture sentiment quickly.

Why it’s not higher:
- Unlike a pure story stock bubble, **AI already has real products, real users, real revenue, and real enterprise demand**.
- The biggest AI spenders are mostly **cash-rich incumbents**, not just fragile speculators.
- Even without AGI, there is likely enough practical value in automation and copilots that some of today’s spending is justified.

So: **45%**.  
In other words, I think a meaningful AI-bubble pop within five years is **a serious risk, but not the base case**. And separately, even if xAI reaches AGI first, that alone doesn’t guarantee a great investment outcome if the valuation you pay already prices that in.

rows.answer:
I don’t have live market data beyond June 2024, so treat this as a structural estimate rather than a real-time market call.

**Best estimate: 45%**

That assumes “the AI bubble bursts” means a **broad 40–60% repricing in AI-linked valuations plus a meaningful funding pullback**, not just a normal correction.

Why the odds are that high:
- **Expectations are very far ahead of proven cash flows**, especially for frontier-model companies.
- **Capex is enormous**; if enterprise ROI lags, markets may conclude the buildout was overdone.
- **AI can succeed technologically and still disappoint investors** if models commoditize and prices collapse.
- **History is unkind to hot general-purpose-tech manias**: railroads, radio, dot-com, clean tech, crypto all saw major shakeouts.

Why I don’t put it above 50%:
- **AI already has real utility and real revenue**, unlike many pure-speculation bubbles.
- **The biggest spenders are cash-rich incumbents** like hyperscalers, not fragile startups alone.
- **Costs are falling fast**, which can let adoption catch up to valuations instead of breaking them.
- **Geopolitical/strategic support** may keep money flowing even through setbacks.

So my single-number view is: **about a 45% chance of a true AI-bubble bust within five years**.

One extra point: for **xAI specifically**, the relevant risk is probably **higher than for the overall AI sector**, because frontier labs are more exposed to valuation resets, dilution, and financing risk than diversified AI beneficiaries.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 55%.**

Assuming “the AI bubble bursts” means a broad sector repricing — roughly a 40%+ drop in AI-linked public/private valuations and a real funding squeeze — I think a burst within five years is **slightly more likely than not**.

Why that high:
- Expectations and capex are enormous relative to still-unproven long-run profits.
- Frontier-model businesses are very capital-intensive, with unclear moats and uncertain margins.
- Open-source progress and big-tech competition can commoditize models fast.
- A recession, higher rates, regulation, copyright losses, or power/compute bottlenecks could trigger a sharp reset.

Why not higher:
- AI already has real utility and real revenue, unlike a purely speculative fad.
- The biggest spenders are cash-rich incumbents, which can keep funding the buildout.
- Geopolitical pressure gives AI investment a strategic floor.

So my base case is **a meaningful valuation/funding reset, not necessarily an “AI winter.”** For frontier-model startups like xAI, the practical bubble risk is probably a bit higher than for AI as a whole. Not investment advice.

**Best estimate: 55%**

I’m using “AI bubble burst” to mean something stronger than normal volatility: a broad valuation reset in AI-linked public/private assets, much tighter funding, and sharp multiple compression.

- **Valuations imply very big outcomes.** A lot of AI pricing seems to assume AGI-like economics or dominant winners.
- **Frontier-model economics are still unproven.** Huge capex, uncertain moats, and likely margin pressure make disappointment plausible.
- **Adoption is real, but ROI may lag the hype.** If enterprise gains arrive slower than expected, sentiment can reverse fast.

- **AI is already useful.** This is not pure vapor; there is real demand and real productivity value.
- **The main spenders are strong.** Cash-rich hyperscalers and governments can keep the cycle going longer than a retail-driven mania.
- **The bubble may deflate gradually.** Expectations can come down without a dramatic “burst.”

rows.answer:
Best estimate: **60%**.

That’s based on information through mid-2024, and I’m defining “burst” as a broad, sustained reset in frontier-AI valuations and funding, not just a normal pullback.

Why I’m above 50%:
- Current frontier-lab valuations imply extremely strong future economics.
- Capex is exploding faster than proven end-user profits.
- Many AI businesses may get commoditized, so even if AI is huge, a lot of the value may go to users, cloud platforms, or incumbents rather than standalone labs.
- Historically, genuinely transformative technologies often still produce investment bubbles that later pop.

Why I’m not higher:
- AI already has real utility, revenue, and adoption.
- The main spenders are cash-rich hyperscalers and governments, which can keep the boom going longer than a retail-driven mania.
- A major capability jump could make today’s spending look justified.

For an **xAI-style** bet, this is the relevant slice of the market, and it’s more bubble-prone than “AI” in general. So if you force me to one number: **60%**.

rows.answer:
Best estimate: **60%**.

I’m interpreting “AI bubble burst” as a **major, sustained repricing of the hottest AI assets**, especially frontier-model startups like xAI and the most AI-dependent public names, not just ordinary volatility.

Why I put it above even odds:
- **Expectations are extremely high.** A lot of valuation assumes very fast monetization and possibly AGI-like progress, while real enterprise adoption usually arrives more slowly.
- **Frontier AI is brutally capital-intensive.** Even if capabilities improve fast, investors may discover that model providers don’t capture the economics because margins get competed away.
- **Private-market valuations look especially fragile.** xAI-relevant pricing is more sentiment-sensitive than the broader tech market.
- **Macro or regulatory shocks could puncture the narrative.** Rates, recession, export controls, copyright rulings, safety regulation, or power/compute constraints could all trigger a reset.
- **History rhymes.** Truly transformative technologies often do have bubbles that burst before the long-run winners fully emerge.

Why I don’t put it higher:
- **AI is real, not fake.** There is already meaningful utility, revenue, and falling cost curves.
- **The ecosystem is backed by cash-rich incumbents.** That makes this sturdier than a classic startup-only mania.
- **Fundamentals could catch up.** Even without AGI, broad deployment across software, search, customer service, coding, and workflow automation could justify a lot of spending.

So my single best estimate is **60%**.

Also: **“first to AGI” is not the same as “best investment.”** Even if xAI wins technically, the value could accrue elsewhere, or the economics could disappoint relative to the price paid.
